html:
[402]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 金陵有林茂才 - Ancient name for Nanjing; 'Mao Cai' refers to a scholar or talent.
  • 寇至陷城中 - '寇' means 'enemy,' '陷城' means 'captured the city.'
  • 羁某馆 - '羁' means 'detained,' '馆' refers to a guesthouse.
  • 胁授伪书记 - '胁' means 'coerced,' '伪书记' means 'false secretary.'
  • 夜阑 - Deep into the night.
  • 刁斗四起 - '刁斗' refers to watchfires or beacons used by soldiers.
  • 悲从中来 - '悲' means 'sadness,' '从中来' means 'arose from within.'
  • 不知此身作何究竟 - '究竟' means 'ultimate fate' or 'outcome.'
  • 眷属有无存没 - '眷属' means 'family,' '存没' means 'alive or dead.'
  • 默诵《金经》不辍 - '金经' refers to the 'Jingang Jing' (Diamond Sutra), a Buddhist scripture.
  • 一夕正诵经 - '一夕' means 'one night.'
  • 闻窗外一叟呼曰 - '叟' means 'old man.'
  • 此时尚不随我三更耶 - '三更' refers to the third watch of the night, roughly 3 AM.
  • 三更者，贼语逋逃者名色也 - '逋逃者' means 'fugitives' or 'those who escape.'
  • 生出户观之 - '出户' means 'went out the door.'
  • 白发叟，布衣冠，古须眉 - '布衣冠' means 'wearing plain clothes and a simple cap.'
  • 素昧平生 - '素昧' means 'never met.'
  • 尔病痴耶 - '病痴' means 'foolish' or 'insane.'
  • 径前揽生袖急走 - '揽袖' means 'grabbed the sleeve.'
  • 飘飘若御风行 - '御风' means 'riding the wind.'
  • 城 - '' refers to the city gates being closed.
  • 鸣钲击柝 - '鸣钲' means 'beating drums,' '击柝' means 'beating wooden clappers.'
  • 贼令森严 - '森严' means 'strict' or 'rigorous.'
  • 巡逻者若无闻见 - '巡逻者' means 'patrol guards.'
  • 狮子山 - Specific location.
  • 挈生猛跳城下 - '挈' means 'grabbed,' '猛跳' means 'leaped.'
  • 若履平地 - '履平地' means 'walking on flat ground.'
  • 江口，若何飞渡 - '江口' means 'river mouth.'
  • 满江红大船 - '满江红' refers to a large red boat.
  • 下关口 - '下关' refers to a specific location.
  • 解缆挂帆 - '解缆' means 'untied the ropes,' '挂帆' means 'raised the sail.'
  • 风瑟瑟 - '瑟瑟' describes the sound of the wind.
  • 直指江北 - '江北' means 'northern bank of the river.'
  • 叟即弄船人也 - '弄船人' means 'boatman.'
  • 生入舱 - '舱' means 'cabin' or 'hold.'
  • 一男一女 - '一男' means 'a man,' '一女' means 'a woman.'
  • 已先在 - '已先在' means 'were already present.'
  • 生之同窗友也 - '同窗友' means 'schoolmate.'
  • 生之邻家娃 - '邻家娃' means 'neighbor's daughter.'
  • 素艳之而未能销魂者也 - '素艳之' means 'always admired,' '未能销魂' means 'had never captivated.'
  • 略寒温 - '寒温' refers to polite greetings.
  • 瞑目坐 - '瞑目' means 'closed his eyes.'
  • 女频视生 - '频视' means 'frequently looked.'
  • 秋波盈盈 - '秋波' refers to the woman's gaze, '盈盈' means 'filled with longing.'
  • 宛似心许 - '宛似' means 'as if,' '心许' means 'agreed in their hearts.'
  • 若碍友不敢言耳 - '碍友' means 'hesitant because of her friend.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 金陵 - Ancient name for Nanjing, a major city in China's history.
  • 寇 - Refers to enemy forces, likely during a period of conflict.

[403]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 時夜色昏黑，山月墜江 - Describes the setting as dark and the moon sinking into the river, creating a somber and isolated atmosphere.
  • 聞友與鄰女，時有調笑聲，漸有褻聲 - Describes the sounds of playful and then increasingly inappropriate conversation between friends and a neighbor’s daughter.
  • 急斂神掩耳，危坐默誦《金經》 - Describes the protagonist’s reaction: he quickly focuses, covers his ears, and sits upright, reciting the *Jing* (a sacred text, likely the *Diamond Sutra* or *Heart Sutra*).
  • 危坐 - Means 'sitting upright' or 'sitting in a dignified manner.'
  • 《金經》 - Likely refers to the *Jing* (a sacred Buddhist text), though the specific text is not explicitly named.
  • 叟在後艄，鼓楫高歌 - Describes an elderly man in the rear of the boat, rowing and singing loudly.
  • 天風浪浪兮，江水粼粼 - A poetic description of the wind and water, evoking a natural yet eerie scene.
  • 月山劍樹兮，雪窖火炕 - A metaphorical description of contrasting extremes (cold vs. heat, light vs. darkness).
  • 懦懦蠢蠢兮，蟲蟲情情 - Describes the protagonist’s inner turmoil and confusion.
  • 何者因何者果兮，絮絮萍萍 - A philosophical or existential reflection on cause and effect, with a sense of aimlessness.
  • 夜何其，夜向晨 - A poetic description of the passage of time from night into dawn.
  • 人鬼有關兮，禍福無門 - A reflection on the boundary between humans and spirits, and the randomness of fortune and misfortune.
  • 袖出一函與之 - Describes the elderly man handing over a letter or document.
  • 崩角在地，泣求姓名 - Describes the protagonist’s desperate reaction, falling to the ground and weeping to learn the elderly man’s identity.
  • 笑而不答 - Describes the elderly man’s enigmatic response.
  • 起視帆影如驶，顷刻若逾金焦 - Describes the boat’s rapid movement, as if it were passing by the Jinshan and Jiaoshan (two famous mountains near Yangzhou).
  • 出海门外矣 - Indicates that the boat has left the Yangtze River and is now in the open sea.
  • 果遇舊雨，挈赴淮安 - Describes the protagonist encountering an old acquaintance and being taken to Huai’an.
  • 視其書 - Describes the protagonist reading the letter.
  • 余本馬姓，生平喜於苦海救人 - The letter reveals the elderly man’s identity as a man surnamed Ma, who has spent his life helping others in their suffering.
  • 爾能端正，不负吾意 - The letter praises the protagonist for his moral integrity.
  • 两个小痴虫，故态复萌，可恶，已将他带赴东海去也 - The letter explains that the elderly man has taken the two young women (likely the neighbor’s daughters) to the East Sea, indicating a moral judgment.
  • 生后入袁端敏公临淮大营，以军功保二千石 - Describes the protagonist’s later career, serving in the army under General Yuan Duanmin and being promoted to a high rank.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夜色昏黑，山月墜江 - Evokes a traditional Chinese literary setting of darkness and isolation, often used to create a mysterious or supernatural atmosphere.
  • 《金經》 - Likely refers to a sacred Buddhist text, such as the *Diamond Sutra* or *Heart Sutra*, symbolizing spiritual discipline and enlightenment.
  • 叟 - Refers to an elderly man, often associated with wisdom or supernatural power in Chinese literature.
  • 天風浪浪兮，江水粼粼 - A poetic description inspired by classical Chinese literature, emphasizing the natural world.
  • 月山劍樹兮，雪窖火炕 - A metaphorical description of contrasting extremes, possibly inspired by Buddhist or Taoist philosophy.
  • 人鬼有關兮，禍福無門 - Reflects the Chinese belief in the interconnectedness of the human and spirit worlds, and the unpredictability of fate.
  • 金焦 - Refers to Jinshan and Jiaoshan, two famous mountains near Yangzhou, often mentioned in Chinese literature as symbols of beauty and history.
  • 袁端敏公 - Refers to General Yuan Duanmin, a historical figure known for his military service during the late Qing dynasty.
  • 临淮大营 - Refers to a military camp in Huai’an, a city in Jiangsu Province, indicating the protagonist’s military career.
  • 二千石 - A high-ranking official title in the Chinese imperial bureaucracy, indicating the protagonist’s success and status.

Proper Nouns

  • 袁端敏公 - General Yuan Duanmin, a historical figure known for his military service during the late Qing dynasty.
  • 临淮大营 - A military camp in Huai’an, a city in Jiangsu Province.
  • 二千石 - A high-ranking official title in the Chinese imperial bureaucracy.
  • 金焦 - Jinshan and Jiaoshan, two famous mountains near Yangzhou.
  • 《金經》 - Likely refers to a sacred Buddhist text, such as the *Diamond Sutra* or *Heart Sutra*.

[404]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 離垢 - Metaphorical name for the garden, emphasizing cleanliness and purity.
  • 治園輔 - To manage a garden; '輔' can also imply a secondary or supportive role.
  • 植花樹以自娱 - To plant flowers and trees for self-entertainment.
  • 痴于洁 - Obsessed with cleanliness; '痴' conveys a sense of obsession or madness.
  • 藩溷亦时时湔滁 - Even the fence and latrine were washed frequently; '藩溷' refers to the garden's boundary and latrine, and '湔滁' means to wash or clean.
  • 见嫉于世 - Hated by the world; '见嫉' conveys the idea of being envied or hated.
  • 百折不回 - Unyielding despite repeated setbacks.
  • 上下夤缘 - To curry favor with superiors and subordinates; '夤缘' implies seeking connections or favors.
  • 私献秦凤爵 - Privately offered a bribe to Qin Fengju; '私献' implies a secret or improper offering.
  • 厚殓僮 - To bury the servant in a lavish manner.
  • 养其父 - To support his father.
  • 婢献羹 - The maid offered soup; '献' implies a formal or respectful offering.
  • 碗炙手 - The bowl was hot; '炙手' conveys the intense heat.
  • 坠地碎 - Fell to the ground and broke.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 离垢园 - The name of the garden, emphasizing cleanliness and purity.
  • 贾名云章，字天孙 - Refers to the owner of the garden, whose name is Yunzhang and courtesy name is Tiansun.
  • 米襄阳爱洁成癖 - Refers to Mi Fu (米芾), a famous painter and calligrapher from the Song Dynasty, known for his obsession with cleanliness.
  • 倪云林嫉俗如仇 - Refers to Ni Zan (倪瓒), a famous painter from the Yuan Dynasty, known for his disdain for the mundane world.
  • 鼎彝书画 - Refers to ancient bronze vessels and paintings, symbolizing high culture and art.
  • 花鸟虫鱼 - Refers to flowers, birds, insects, and fish, common subjects in traditional Chinese art.
  • 许桥 - The son of the owner, whose name is Xuqiao.
  • 蓝氏 - The wife of Xuqiao, whose surname is Lan.
  • 秦凤爵 - A high-ranking official, likely a bribe recipient.

Proper Nouns

  • 离垢园 - The name of the garden.
  • 天孙 - The courtesy name of the owner.
  • 许桥 - The son of the owner.
  • 蓝氏 - The wife of Xuqiao.
  • 秦凤爵 - A high-ranking official.

[405]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 供秦制 - Refers to something conforming to the strict standards or customs of the Qin dynasty.
  • 命根 - Metaphor for something essential or vital.
  • 断吾命根 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'cutting off my life root,' implying a severe or life-threatening action.
  • 呼杖 - Idiomatic expression indicating a demand for punishment or discipline.
  • 厚殓之 - Euphemism for providing a lavish funeral.
  • 召僧讽经超荐 - Ritual practice for the benefit of the deceased, involving summoning monks to recite sutras.
  • 个男儿 - Colloquial or informal way of referring to someone.
  • 毫无一点情 - Indicating a complete lack of emotion or empathy.
  • 侬见之 - Colloquial expression meaning 'I saw him.'
  • 几欲坠唾 - Indicating extreme disgust.
  • 倒好耍子 - Informal expression suggesting enjoyment.
  • 袖出一圈 - Metaphorical expression suggesting something is concealed and then revealed.
  • 若不改悟 - Conditional phrase suggesting a need for change.
  • 恐随身皆荆棘场 - Metaphor for a life filled with difficulties.
  • 尚得为离垢园 - Metaphor for a life free from impurities or troubles.
  • 刺刺不休 - Indicating persistent and annoying criticism.
  • 稍讽之 - Gentle form of rebuke.
  • 怒以砚击友 - Angrily hitting a friend with an inkstone, indicating a violent reaction.
  • 几碎其颅 - Hyperbolic expression indicating a severe injury.
  • 以炙炭煮汉玉佩 - Destructive act of boiling a Han dynasty jade pendant with hot charcoal.
  • 火起，蔓延邻舍 - Fire started and spread to neighboring houses, indicating a fire that spread beyond its original location.
  • 风号焰烈 - Vivid description of a raging fire, often used in literature to depict chaos and destruction.
  • 顷刻数家 - In a moment, several houses, indicating a rapid spread of damage.
  • 婉谢厚偿 - Polite way of refusing generous compensation.
  • 无恙 - Indicating that no damage was done.
  • 背父 - Opposition to one's father.
  • 蓄温水 - Preparing warm water, often associated with ritualistic or ceremonial acts.
  • 澡身更衣薰香 - Ritualistic act of bathing, changing clothes, and perfuming oneself, often seen in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 作种种恶态 - Displaying negative behavior, often associated with moral corruption.
  • 哀啼至夜 - Prolonged mourning, often seen in traditional Chinese funerary practices.
  • 美妇人 - Beautiful woman, often used in literature to describe a character or a metaphorical figure.
  • 姊姊冤杀哉 - Expression of sympathy and accusation, often used in traditional Chinese literature to convey emotional depth.
  • 此中大有佳处 - Positive assessment, often used to describe a place or situation.
  • 对镜匀粉黛 - Applying makeup in front of the mirror, often used in literature to describe a ritualistic act of beautification.
  • 整衣衫 - Neatly arranging clothes, often used to describe preparation for an occasion.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贾 - Common surname in Chinese culture, possibly referring to a specific historical figure or a generic merchant.
  • 秦制 - Refers to the standards or customs of the Qin dynasty, known for its strict legalism and centralization.
  • 砚 - Inkstone used in traditional Chinese calligraphy, often associated with scholarly pursuits.
  • 汉玉佩 - Jade pendant from the Han dynasty, symbolizing status and beauty.
  • 离垢园 - Metaphorical term for a pure and trouble-free life.
  • 风号焰烈 - Vivid description of a raging fire, often used in literature to depict chaos and destruction.
  • 婉谢厚偿 - Polite way of refusing generous compensation, often seen in social interactions.
  • 蓄温水 - Preparing warm water, often associated with ritualistic or ceremonial acts.
  • 澡身更衣薰香 - Ritualistic act of bathing, changing clothes, and perfuming oneself, often seen in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 作种种恶态 - Displaying negative behavior, often associated with moral corruption.
  • 哀啼至夜 - Prolonged mourning, often seen in traditional Chinese funerary practices.
  • 美妇人 - Beautiful woman, often used in literature to describe a character or a metaphorical figure.
  • 姊姊冤杀哉 - Expression of sympathy and accusation, often used in traditional Chinese literature to convey emotional depth.

[406]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 婦以羅帶系梁上 - A woman ties a silk sash to the beam, a common method of suicide in classical Chinese literature.
  • 對妻再拜 - A formal act of bowing twice, often used to express deep respect or sorrow.
  • 逡巡自缢 - "逡巡" means to hesitate or delay, often used to describe a moment of indecision before taking a drastic action.
  • 撫尸號痛 - To hold the body and wail in grief.
  • 死而后苏 - To die and then revive, often used in a hyperbolic sense to describe extreme emotional distress.
  • 猶以衣袖擦宣炉 - "擦宣炉" means to wipe a bronze incense burner, a metaphor for suppressing one's emotions.
  • 聊滴清泪而已 - To shed a few clear tears, a restrained expression of grief.
  • 县役到门，汹汹如虎 - The county officials arrive at the door, behaving like tigers, indicating their aggressive and intimidating presence.
  • 呼号啖嚼 - To shout and chew, a vivid description of the officials' rough and disrespectful behavior.
  • 邻人来观，乱摘花果 - Neighbors come to watch, picking flowers and fruits indiscriminately, showing their lack of respect and decorum.
  • 痛极失声 - To lose one's voice from extreme pain.
  • 叟疑渠尚有遗之悲 - The father-in-law suspects that the deceased still has unresolved grief.
  • 又以许桥重贿，始忍而罢讼 - The father-in-law, bribed by Xu Qiao, finally relents and ends the lawsuit.
  • 然家道亦从此中落矣 - However, the family's fortunes decline from this point on.
  • 烂铜碎玉，昔以重价购来，顷以廉资售出 - Once valuable copper and jade, now sold at low prices, indicating the family's financial decline.
  • 炊烟渐断，仅剩荒园 - The smoke from cooking fires gradually disappears, leaving only a desolate garden.
  • 贾鳏而子未婚，时作楚囚之泣 - Jia is a widower, and his son is unmarried, often weeping like a Chu captive, a metaphor for deep sorrow.
  • 忽有客携短童来谒，仙风道骨，翩翩在门 - A guest arrives with a young boy, exuding an ethereal and scholarly demeanor.
  • 迓入与谈，自言张姓无字，人呼为张老相公 - Jia welcomes him in and they talk; the guest introduces himself as Zhang, surname only, and is called "Zhang Laoshengong."
  • 渊博古今，尘谈霏屑 - Scholarly and knowledgeable about both ancient and modern times, engaging in trivial conversation.
  • 贾恨相见晚 - Jia regrets meeting him too late.
  • 许桥思留以慰父，以敝裘付质库，置酒篝灯，且留下榻 - Xu Qiao thinks to keep him to comfort his father, selling his old fur coat to the pawnshop, setting up a feast with lanterns, and inviting him to stay.
  • 自贾之好洁也，君子恶之，小人诳之 - Since Jia's obsession with cleanliness, gentlemen dislike him, and small people deceive him.
  • 富易为贫，朋侪绝迹 - From rich to poor, friends disappear.
  • 顷得张，不禁狂喜 - Upon meeting Zhang, Jia cannot help but be ecstatic.
  • 久之，张唾落于壁，必怒于心 - After a while, Zhang spits on the wall, which angers Jia.
  • 遗屙于庭，必怒于色 - Zhang defecates in the courtyard, which makes Jia visibly angry.
  • 一日勉与坐，张之童又弄毙架上鹦鹉，怒遂不可忍 - One day, Jia forces himself to sit with Zhang, but Zhang's child plays and kills a parrot on the perch, making Jia's anger unbearable.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 罗带 - A silk sash, often used in classical literature to symbolize beauty and elegance.
  • 梁 - The beam of a house, often used in suicide scenes.
  • 再拜 - A formal act of bowing twice, often used to express deep respect or sorrow.
  • 逡巡 - To hesitate or delay, often used to describe a moment of indecision before taking a drastic action.
  • 抚尸号痛 - A common expression of grief in classical literature.
  • 死而后苏 - A hyperbolic expression of extreme emotional distress.
  • 擦宣炉 - Wiping a bronze incense burner, a metaphor for suppressing one's emotions.
  • 滴清泪 - Shedding a few clear tears, a restrained expression of grief.
  • 讣岳 - Informing the father-in-law of a death, a common practice in classical Chinese society.
  • 蓝叟 - The father-in-law, often a respected figure in classical literature.
  • 县役 - County officials, often depicted as aggressive and intimidating.
  • 呼号啖嚼 - Shouting and chewing, a vivid description of the officials' rough and disrespectful behavior.
  • 邻人来观，乱摘花果 - Neighbors coming to watch, picking flowers and fruits indiscriminately, showing their lack of respect and decorum.
  • 痛极失声 - Losing one's voice from extreme pain, a common expression of grief.
  • 叟疑渠尚有遗之悲 - The father-in-law suspects that the deceased still has unresolved grief.
  • 许桥重贿 - Xu Qiao's bribe, a common practice in classical Chinese society to influence officials.
  • 烂铜碎玉 - Once valuable copper and jade, now sold at low prices, indicating the family's financial decline.
  • 炊烟渐断 - The smoke from cooking fires gradually disappearing, leaving only a desolate garden.
  • 贾鳏而子未婚 - Jia is a widower, and his son is unmarried, often weeping like a Chu captive, a metaphor for deep sorrow.
  • 仙风道骨 - Exuding an ethereal and scholarly demeanor, often used to describe a wise and respected figure.
  • 翩翩在门 - Graceful and elegant, often used to describe a respected guest.
  • 渊博古今 - Scholarly and knowledgeable about both ancient and modern times.
  • 尘谈霏屑 - Engaging in trivial conversation, often used to describe a casual and pleasant interaction.
  • 贾恨相见晚 - Jia regrets meeting him too late, a common expression of regret in classical literature.
  • 许桥思留以慰父 - Xu Qiao thinks to keep him to comfort his father, a common practice in classical Chinese society.
  • 敝裘付质库 - Selling his old fur coat to the pawnshop, a common practice in times of financial distress.
  • 置酒篝灯 - Setting up a feast with lanterns, a common practice in classical Chinese society to welcome guests.
  • 自贾之好洁也，君子恶之，小人诳之 - Since Jia's obsession with cleanliness, gentlemen dislike him, and small people deceive him, a common theme in classical literature.
  • 富易为贫，朋侪绝迹 - From rich to poor, friends disappear, a common theme in classical literature.
  • 顷得张，不禁狂喜 - Upon meeting Zhang, Jia cannot help but be ecstatic, a common expression of joy in classical literature.
  • 久之，张唾落于壁，必怒于心 - After a while, Zhang spits on the wall, which angers Jia, a common expression of anger in classical literature.
  • 遗屙于庭，必怒于色 - Zhang defecates in the courtyard, which makes Jia visibly angry, a common expression of anger in classical literature.
  • 一日勉与坐，张之童又弄毙架上鹦鹉，怒遂不可忍 - One day, Jia forces himself to sit with Zhang, but Zhang's child plays and kills a parrot on the perch, making Jia's anger unbearable, a common expression of anger in classical literature.

[407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 莫怒莫怒 - Colloquial expression meaning 'don't be angry' or 'calm down,' often used to reassure someone.
  • 携君游一胜境以赎罪 - Phrase meaning 'take you to a beautiful place to atone for your sin,' where '赎罪' (to atone for a sin) is a formal expression.
  • 豁然开朗 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'suddenly clear and bright,' often used to describe a sudden realization or a pleasant surprise.
  • 苍松夹道 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'pine trees lining the path,' creating a scenic image.
  • 流水涓涓 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'gentle flowing water,' evoking a serene atmosphere.
  • 白玉为梁 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'white jade beams,' suggesting elegance and luxury.
  • 幽芳作帧 - Metaphorical phrase meaning 'fragrant flowers as frames,' creating a poetic image.
  • 两壁磨崖上镌科蚪奇字 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'carved strange characters on the walls,' where '镌' (to engrave) and '科蚪奇字' (strange characters) suggest ancient and mysterious inscriptions.
  • 似秦汉以上书 - Phrase meaning 'resembling writings from before the Qin and Han dynasties,' indicating the age and style of the inscriptions.
  • 逾岭一重 - Phrase meaning 'crossing another mountain range,' suggesting a journey through multiple landscapes.
  • 薜荔五色 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'five-colored climbing plants,' evoking a vibrant and natural scene.
  • 彩丝下垂 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'colorful threads hanging down,' creating a vivid image.
  • 插架牙笺 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'sheets of fine paper inserted into bookshelves,' suggesting a well-organized and scholarly environment.
  • 间以丝竹 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'interspersed with string and bamboo instruments,' indicating the presence of music.
  • 炉香犹篆 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'incense still forming spirals,' suggesting a lingering and pleasant aroma.
  • 帘波有纹 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'curtains with wavy patterns,' creating a visual image of elegance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贾 - Refers to a wealthy merchant or businessman, likely the protagonist of the story.

  • 离垢园 - 'Ligou Garden,' a place where the protagonist plans to build a shrine to Zhang Sanfeng.
  • 张公 - A respectful term for Master Zhang, likely referring to Zhang Sanfeng.
  • 仙人 - 'Immortal,' a term used in Taoism to describe enlightened beings who have transcended the cycle of birth and death.
  • 邋遢 - A nickname for Zhang Sanfeng, meaning 'untidy' or 'careless,' but in this context, it refers to his unconventional and unorthodox nature.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '架上鹦鹉如故，曰：“茶来，主人醒也。”' is straightforward but requires attention to the parrot's role as a symbol of continuity and routine.
  • The sentence '乃泣告子，使人侦之，邻家栅中，果生六豕，一颠痫遽毙。' is elliptical and requires careful unpacking to convey the sequence of events: the protagonist weeps and tells his son, who then investigates and finds that six pigs were born, one of which died suddenly.
  • The phrase '所谓芭蕉者，庭中白菜一畦也。' is ambiguous and requires interpretation. It likely means 'what is called the banana plant is actually a patch of Chinese cabbage in the courtyard.'
  • The sentence '地狱已在眼前，奈何不悟？' is a rhetorical question, emphasizing the protagonist's realization of the consequences of his actions.
  • The sentence '两孙举于乡，泥金到门，贾犹捻珠讽诵。' is compact and requires careful parsing to convey the sequence of events: the two grandsons pass the imperial examination, and gold-leafed scrolls arrive at the door, but the protagonist continues to pray and recite scriptures.

  • 恽氏 - The wife of the protagonist, described as beautiful and virtuous.
  • 张公 - A respectful term for Master Zhang, likely referring to Zhang Sanfeng.
  • 离垢园 - 'Ligou Garden,' a place where the protagonist plans to build a shrine.
  • 张邋遢 - Zhang Lata, a nickname for Zhang Sanfeng, a legendary figure in Chinese martial arts and Taoism.

  • 缕述陶庄事 - Detailing the events of Taozhuang, indicating a recounting of past events.
  • 陶自毛去后 - Since Mr. Mao left, Taozhuang, referring to the period after Mr. Mao's departure.

  • 幕游嵫水 - Touring as a guest in the Zisi River area.

  • 贫为当道佣书 - Poor, working as a copyist for officials; '当道' (dāng dào) refers to officials or those in power.

  • 撇堂上白头 - Leaving her elderly parents behind; '白头' (bái tóu) refers to elderly parents.
  • 母素审贞女贤 - The mother, knowing the girl's virtue; '素审' (sù shěn) means 'always knew' or 'always recognized.'

  • 梦有儒衣冠拜于门外者 - Means 'I dreamt of a scholar in official attire bowing outside the door,' indicating a dream vision.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一夜夢天女 - Literally 'a night dream of a celestial maiden,' referring to a dream in which a heavenly woman appears.
  • 冉冉降中庭 - "冉冉" means 'slowly' or 'gradually,' and "降中庭" means 'descend into the courtyard.' Together, this phrase describes the maiden's gentle descent into the courtyard.
  • 以丹授之曰 - "授之" means 'gave it to her,' and "曰" introduces the maiden's words. The phrase indicates the maiden giving a pill to the dreamer.
  • 吾天孫也 - "天孫" means 'granddaughter of heaven,' a title often used for celestial beings.
  • 怜子贞孝且有疾 - "怜子" means 'pity you,' "贞孝" means 'loyal and filial,' and "有疾" means 'sick.' This phrase explains the maiden's reason for giving the pill.
  • 携奉神丹 - "携奉" means 'bring and present,' and "神丹" means 'divine pill.' Together, this phrase refers to the maiden bringing a divine pill.
  • 子其吞之 - "子" means 'you,' "其" is a particle indicating command, and "吞之" means 'swallow it.' This phrase is a command to swallow the pill.
  • 再拜裣衽而后服 - "再拜" means 'bow twice,' "裣衽" means 'lower one's skirt,' and "而后服" means 'and then swallow.' This phrase describes the dreamer's respectful actions before swallowing the pill.
  • 神女去 - "神女" means 'celestial maiden,' and "去" means 'leave.' This phrase indicates the maiden leaving.
  • 女自觉心遽安而疴若失 - "女" means 'the woman,' "自觉" means 'feel,' "心遽安" means 'suddenly feel at ease,' and "疴若失" means 'as if the illness has vanished.' This phrase describes the woman feeling better after swallowing the pill.
  • 翌再买丝挑线 - "翌" means 'next day,' "再买丝" means 'buy silk again,' and "挑线" means 'pick up sewing.' This phrase indicates the woman resuming her work the next day.
  • 功倍往时 - "功" means 'work,' "倍" means 'twice as much,' and "往时" means 'as before.' This phrase means the woman's work is twice as productive as before.
  • 姑又病 - "姑" means 'mother-in-law,' and "又病" means 'fall ill again.' This phrase indicates the mother-in-law falling ill again.
  • 女衣不解带者数月 - "女衣" means 'the woman (in her role as a daughter-in-law),' "不解带" means 'not remove her belt,' and "者数月" means 'for several months.' This phrase describes the woman staying by her mother-in-law's side for several months.
  • 殆弥留时 - "殆" means 'almost,' and "弥留时" means 'on her deathbed.' This phrase indicates the mother-in-law is near death.
  • 吾不幸有殇子，何幸而有贞妇耶 - "吾不幸" means 'unfortunately,' "有殇子" means 'have a deceased child,' "何幸" means 'what a blessing,' and "有贞妇耶" means 'to have a loyal wife.' This phrase expresses the mother-in-law's gratitude for having a loyal daughter-in-law.
  • 吾年来得汝调养，胜于若在时也 - "吾年来" means 'these years,' "得汝调养" means 'with your care,' "胜于" means 'better than,' and "若在时也" means 'if he were alive.' This phrase indicates that the mother-in-law feels better cared for than she would have been if her son were alive.
  • 今已矣，行将告尔舅尔夫于地下矣 - "今已矣" means 'now it is over,' "行将" means 'will soon,' "告尔舅尔夫" means 'inform your father-in-law and husband,' and "于地下" means 'in the afterlife.' This phrase indicates the mother-in-law's impending death and her desire to inform her in-laws in the afterlife.
  • 吾儿幸依阿母活，毋以我为念 - "吾儿" means 'my child,' "幸依阿母活" means 'hopefully live with my mother-in-law,' and "毋以我为念" means 'do not worry about me.' This phrase is the mother-in-law's final words to her daughter-in-law.
  • 言已，目遽瞑 - "言已" means 'after speaking,' and "目遽瞑" means 'her eyes closed suddenly.' This phrase indicates the mother-in-law's death.
  • 女毁瘠号痛，泪坠成冰 - "女毁瘠" means 'the woman mourns and loses weight,' "号痛" means 'cries in pain,' and "泪坠成冰" means 'tears fall like ice.' This phrase describes the woman's intense grief.
  • 其母恐女殉，预往防闲之 - "其母" means 'her mother,' "恐女殉" means 'fear the woman will commit suicide,' "预往" means 'go ahead,' and "防闲之" means 'prevent her.' This phrase indicates the mother's concern and preventive action.
  • 女泣邀四邻坐堂上 - "女泣" means 'the woman cries,' "邀四邻" means 'invite neighbors,' and "坐堂上" means 'sit in the hall.' This phrase describes the woman inviting neighbors to the hall.
  • 奉母坐屋隅 - "奉母" means 'with her mother,' "坐" means 'sit,' and "屋隅" means 'in a corner of the house.' This phrase indicates the woman's mother sitting in a corner of the house.
  • 稽首曰 - "稽首" means 'bow deeply,' and "曰" introduces the woman's words. This phrase describes the woman bowing deeply and speaking.
  • 吾夫死，吾姑又死矣 - "吾夫" means 'my husband,' "死" means 'died,' and "吾姑又死矣" means 'my mother-in-law also died.' This phrase indicates the woman's husband and mother-in-law both died.
  • 族无接续之绪 - "族" means 'clan,' "无" means 'no,' "接续" means 'continuation,' and "之绪" means 'of the lineage.' This phrase indicates the clan has no one to continue the lineage.
  • 家无期功之亲 - "家" means 'family,' "无" means 'no,' "期功" means 'close relatives,' and "之亲" means 'of the family.' This phrase indicates the family has no close relatives.
  • 留此数椽 - "留" means 'leave,' "此" means 'this,' "数椽" means 'several rooms.' This phrase indicates leaving the house.
  • 下及什物 - "下" means 'down to,' "及" means 'including,' "什物" means 'miscellaneous items.' This phrase indicates including miscellaneous items.
  • 何所用乎 - "何" means 'what,' "所用" means 'use,' and "乎" is a particle. This phrase means 'what use is there for them?'
  • 拟乞诸长者 - "拟" means 'plan,' "乞" means 'ask,' "诸" means 'various,' and "长者" means 'elders.' This phrase indicates planning to ask the elders.
  • 代为全售 - "代" means 'on behalf of,' "为" means 'to,' "全售" means 'sell everything.' This phrase means selling everything on behalf of the family.
  • 为吾姑殓葬费用 - "为" means 'for,' "吾姑" means 'my mother-in-law,' "殓葬" means 'funeral,' and "费用" means 'expenses.' This phrase indicates using the money for the mother-in-law's funeral expenses.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天女 - Refers to a celestial maiden, a common figure in Chinese mythology and literature.
  • 神丹 - Refers to a divine pill, often associated with immortality or healing in Chinese folklore.
  • 贞孝 - Refers to loyalty and filial piety, core virtues in Confucian ethics.
  • 殉 - Refers to the act of committing suicide to follow a loved one, a practice that was sometimes observed in ancient China.
  • 四邻 - Refers to the neighbors, who often played a role in community support and social networks.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 遂盡以其資，營齋奠，就窀穸 - 遂 means 'then' or 'thereupon,' indicating a sequence of actions. 尽以其资 means 'used all of their resources.' 营斋奠 refers to preparing offerings and memorial services. 就窀穸 means 'to proceed to the burial site.'
  • 女向母拜別，意將雉經 - 拜别 means 'to bid farewell with a bow.' 雉经 is an archaic term for self-sacrifice, often interpreted as hanging oneself.
  • 由是女始大歸，永作依母計 - 大归 refers to returning home after a marriage, often with the implication of a widow returning to her natal home. 永作依母计 means 'to always rely on her mother's plan.'
  • 清明寒食，則以一盂麥飯，哭於徐氏墓道 - 清明寒食 refers to the Qingming and Hanshi festivals, traditional Chinese holidays for honoring ancestors. 以一盂麥飯 means 'with a bowl of wheat porridge.' 哭於徐氏墓道 means 'to weep at the tomb path of the Xu family.'
  • 女每號恸，長河之水，則呜咽不流 - 每號恸 means 'every time she wailed.' 长河之水 refers to a river, often symbolizing the flow of time or life. 呜咽不流 means 'to sob and not flow,' a metaphor for the river's sorrow.
  • 其族叔某，時劝女再适 - 族叔 means 'a cousin's uncle.' 时劝女再适 means 'often advised her to remarry.'
  • 女大声屏逐之，不容其刺刺语 - 大声屏逐之 means 'shouted loudly to drive him away.' 不容其刺刺语 means 'would not allow him to persist in his talk.'
  • 旧居老屋，为秋风所破，修葺殊难 - 为秋风所破 means 'was destroyed by the autumn wind.' 修葺殊难 means 'was very difficult to repair.'
  • 时河北中大王庙比丘尼某，亦以名媛披剃，素精戒律，永谢俗尘者 - 名媛 means 'a renowned lady.' 披剃 means 'to take monastic vows.' 素精戒律 means 'always excelled in precepts.' 永谢俗尘 means 'permanently renounced the world.'
  • 女与谂熟 - 谂熟 means 'to be well acquainted.'
  • 且先有嫠妇刘妪，亦因前妻子不孝，携己生一幼子、一幼女居焉 - 嫠妇 means 'a widow.' 前妻子 means 'stepchildren.' 携己生一幼子、一幼女居焉 means 'took her own young son and daughter to live there.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 雉经 - An archaic term for self-sacrifice, often interpreted as hanging oneself, which was a form of ritual suicide in ancient China.
  • 大归 - Refers to a widow returning to her natal home, a common practice in traditional Chinese society.
  • 清明寒食 - Traditional Chinese festivals for honoring ancestors, with specific rituals and customs.
  • 比丘尼 - A female Buddhist monk, indicating a religious context.
  • 名媛 - A term for a renowned or distinguished lady, often associated with social status and virtue.
  • 永谢俗尘 - A phrase indicating a permanent renunciation of worldly life, often used in the context of monastic vows.

Proper Nouns

  • 河北中大王庙 - The name of a temple in Hebei province.
  • 刘妪 - The name of a widow.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes the setting and the action, where '已同至浅水' sets the location, and '见幼子方徘徊岸侧' describes the action of seeing the child.
  • This quote from Liu is a complex statement, where '儿来儿来' is a call, '儿生必虏' is a prediction of a grim fate, '即免，兄嫂亦不可久居' is a conditional statement, and '盖相从地下' is a final statement about the afterlife.
  • This quote from the nun is a simple expression of approval, where '善哉善哉' is repeated for emphasis, and '是方不愧清静优婆夷也' is a statement of moral purity.
  • This sentence describes the event and its witness, where '于是六人一齐投水死' describes the action, and '此胁从者所亲见也' identifies the witnesses.
  • This sentence describes the reaction of the townspeople, where '贼' sets the context, '邑之人士闻之' describes the reaction, and '虽未请旌，莫不曰‘贞女贞女’云' describes the specific reaction.
  • This long sentence describes a series of events, where '又数年' sets the time, '一皖南士人，偶至贞女死所' describes the arrival, '临流瞻眺' describes the action, '忽见一美女子，哭立水面' describes the appearance, '风鬟雾鬓，仪态万方' describes the appearance, '手捧赤莲花，冉冉行且歌' describes the actions, and '歌曰：采莲复采叶，骨似寒冰心似铁；犹记湘垒毕命时，鱼不敢吞，龙不' describes the song.

  • 湘垒 - The Xiang River, a river in Hunan Province, often associated with tragic stories and legends.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乞食廛市间 - Literally 'begging for food in the market,' referring to a beggar who solicits alms.
  • 观者围之如堵墙 - A vivid metaphor comparing the crowd of onlookers to a wall, emphasizing the large number of people gathered.
  • 盖其人顶生一角 - '盖' means 'it turns out' or 'as it happens,' introducing the explanation of the beggar's unusual feature.
  • 矗正中 - '矗' means 'standing upright,' emphasizing the prominence and central position of the horn.
  • 朽如蠹啮 - '朽' means 'decayed,' and '蠹啮' means 'eaten by insects,' describing the worn-out and damaged state of the horn.
  • 泫然曰 - '泫然' means 'tearfully' or 'with tears in his eyes,' indicating the emotional state of the beggar.
  • 幼不肖 - '不肖' means 'unworthy' or 'disobedient,' referring to the beggar's misbehavior in childhood.
  • 动辄忤双亲 - '动辄' means 'often' or 'frequently,' and '忤' means 'to defy' or 'to offend,' indicating frequent disobedience to parents.
  • 攫资宿妓 - '攫资' means 'to seize money,' and '宿妓' means 'to stay with prostitutes,' describing the beggar's immoral behavior.
  • 家贫，双亲啖糠而已 - '啖糠' means 'to eat bran,' indicating the impoverished condition of the beggar's family.
  • 犹携酒肴就妓家食 - '携酒肴' means 'to bring wine and food,' and '就妓家食' means 'to go to the brothel to eat,' showing the beggar's continued immoral lifestyle.
  • 尤喜煎炒脆炸，浓汁厚味 - Describes the beggar's preference for fried and rich foods.
  • 亲死，以芦包葬东郭 - '以芦包' means 'wrapped in reeds,' and '东郭' means 'eastern suburb,' describing the simple burial of the beggar's parents.
  • 甫归家，正遭回禄 - '甫' means 'just,' and '回禄' is an ancient term for fire, indicating that the beggar's house was burned down immediately after returning home.
  • 贫不能耐，则于亲族友党中唆是非，挑械斗，兴词讼 - '唆是非' means 'to stir up trouble,' '挑械斗' means 'to provoke fights,' and '兴词讼' means 'to initiate lawsuits,' describing the beggar's manipulative and disruptive behavior.
  • 己则假为调停，攫鹬蚌利 - '假为调停' means 'to pretend to mediate,' and '攫鹬蚌利' means 'to seize the advantage of the dispute,' indicating the beggar's cunning and self-serving actions.
  • 是年，年三十有二，忽病，自分必死 - '自分必死' means 'thought he would surely die,' indicating the beggar's belief that he was dying.
  • 数日，颅肉痒甚，抓挠肤裂，紫血涔涔 - Describes the beggar's physical symptoms and the severity of his condition.
  • 突一角出，如笋掀泥 - '如笋掀泥' means 'like a bamboo shoot emerging from the mud,' describing the sudden appearance of the horn.
  • 自顾镜中，已似神羊 - '神羊' means 'divine sheep,' referring to the mythological creature with a single horn, the unicorn.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 回禄 - An ancient term for fire, often used in classical texts to describe fires.
  • 神羊 - Refers to the mythological unicorn, a symbol of purity and divine power in Chinese mythology.

  • 十余日 - Means 'more than ten days,' indicating the duration of the suffering.

  • 痛果已 - Means 'the pain indeed subsided,' indicating the result of the confession.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • While '而奇痛不可忍，呼号床第间' describes the intensity of the pain and the physical reaction, it must be read as a single unit.
  • '十余日，忽梦一神人告之曰' introduces a supernatural element and must be read as a single unit.
  • '尔思病从何来？角从何生？曷言其故，痛自己' is a series of rhetorical questions that must be read as a single unit, emphasizing the cause-and-effect relationship.
  • '寐而自省，试以己之罪恶，于人前姑言之' describes the process of self-reflection and confession, which must be read as a single unit.
  • '痛果已，顷年已六十，一日不言，一日便痛' describes the results of the confession and the ongoing condition, which must be read as a single unit.
  • '唏嘘泪下如雨，人怜之，多施一文钱' describes the emotional state and the community's response, which must be read as a single unit.
  • '乡中小儿，偶摩娑其角而动摇之，便哀呼诵佛号' describes the interaction with children and the religious response, which must be read as a single unit.
  • '自云：“兴化东乡人，无名氏，惟号独角兽”' provides a self-introduction, which must be read as a single unit.
  • '余戏为摹小像，赞云' introduces the author's response, which must be read as a single unit.
  • '彼何人斯，乞于东郭；身未披毛，首先戴角；春笋怒芽，上指寥廓' is a series of poetic descriptions that must be read as a single unit, emphasizing the unique qualities of the subject.
  • '其身犹人，其心则兽；兽耶人耶，峨峨穿透' highlights the duality of the subject's nature, which must be read as a single unit.
  • '地狱人间，黄泉白昼；非獬豸冠，亦非角端' suggests a liminal existence and unique status, which must be read as a single unit.
  • '铙鼎不铸，山经不刊；好角逐者，蓦见心寒' emphasizes the subject's uniqueness and the impact on others, which must be read as a single unit.

  • 山经 - The *Shan Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas).

  • 先觉求其指示 - Xianjue asked for his guidance (a request for advice or direction).
  • 旧仆再拜曰 - An old servant bowed twice and said (再拜 is a formal greeting, showing respect).
  • 先尊与太夫人 - My late father and mother (先尊 refers to the deceased father, and 太夫人 refers to the deceased mother).
  • 悲痛甚 - Were very sorrowful (a description of their emotional state).
  • 四处招寻 - Searched everywhere (a description of the family's efforts to find Xianjue).
  • 相继而逝 - Passed away one after another (a description of their deaths).
  • 大公子扶柩旋里 - The eldest son carried the coffin back home (扶柩 means to carry the coffin, and 旋里 means to return home).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 武林之灵隐寺 - Lingyin Temple is a famous Buddhist temple in Hangzhou, known for its historical and cultural significance.
  • 大唐 - The Tang Dynasty (618–907 CE) is often regarded as a period of great cultural and artistic achievement in Chinese history.
  • 香积厨 - The Fragrant Heap is a metaphorical name for a temple kitchen, often associated with the Buddha's teachings and the preparation of vegetarian food.
  • 前缘 - The concept of karmic connections or past lives is central to Buddhist and Taoist beliefs.
  • 执爨 - A metaphorical term for the role of a temple kitchen worker, often associated with humility and service.
  • 扶柩旋里 - A traditional practice in Chinese culture where the eldest son carries the coffin of a deceased parent back home for burial.

Proper Nouns

  • 灵隐寺 - Lingyin Temple, a famous Buddhist temple in Hangzhou.
  • 大唐 - The Tang Dynasty, a historical period in Chinese history.
  • 香积厨 - The Fragrant Heap, a metaphorical name for a temple kitchen.
  • 先觉 - The name of the protagonist, Xianjue.
  • 先尊与太夫人 - The deceased father and mother of Xianjue.
  • 大公子 - The eldest son of Xianjue's family.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 缘先太尊在日 - Classical way of referring to a deceased parent, using '在日' to mean 'during his lifetime.'
  • 曾为公子聘冯御史女 - '聘' means to arrange a marriage, and '公子' is a respectful term for a young male member of the aristocracy.
  • 今已及笄 - '及笄' is a traditional term for a girl reaching the age of 15, when she would traditionally have her hair pinned up in a ceremony marking her eligibility for marriage.
  • 催完姻者屡矣 - '完姻' means to complete the marriage rites.
  • 大公子遣奴辈多人相觅 - '相觅' means to search for someone.
  • 先觉知父母已过，大恸几绝 - '已过' means 'have passed away,' and '大恸几绝' is an expression of extreme sorrow.
  • 劝之易服毕姻 - '易服' refers to changing into formal wedding attire, and '毕姻' means to complete the marriage ceremony.
  • 我与汝了此一段姻缘，数乃前定 - '数乃前定' is a common expression meaning 'fate was set beforehand.'
  • 但我已证罗汉果，不通人道 - '证罗汉果' is a Buddhist term for attaining the state of an Arhat, one of the highest levels of spiritual attainment in Theravāda Buddhism.
  • 留此无益 - A concise expression of futility or lack of purpose.
  • 不别而行 - A common expression in Classical Chinese to indicate a departure without farewell.
  • 北走燕山之西域寺 - '北走' means to travel northward; '西域寺' is a term for a Western Buddhist temple, possibly referring to a monastery in the Western Regions (modern Xinjiang or Central Asia).
  • 爱其岩洞幽寂 - A descriptive phrase expressing preference for a secluded place.
  • 面溪背壁，趺坐于中 - '趺坐' is a term for the lotus position used in meditation.
  • 日有寺内人来给食 - '给食' is a formal term for providing food.
  • 坐逾两载，功益精进 - '功益精进' means his spiritual progress became increasingly advanced.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 先太尊 - A respectful term for one’s late father, often used in classical Chinese to refer to deceased parents in a respectful and formal manner.
  • 冯御史 - Refers to a high-ranking official who held the title of *yushi* (御史), a type of censor or inspector in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 及笄 - A traditional coming-of-age ceremony for women, marking the age of 15 and eligibility for marriage.
  • 罗汉果 - A term from Buddhist tradition, referring to the attainment of *arhat* (罗汉), one of the highest spiritual achievements in Theravāda Buddhism, signifying liberation from the cycle of rebirth.
  • 西域寺 - A term for a Western Buddhist temple, likely referring to a monastery in the Western Regions (Xiyu), which in classical Chinese often referred to Central Asia or regions west of China, where Buddhism was introduced from India.
  • 燕山 - A mountain range in northern China, specifically in modern-day Hebei Province, often associated with northern frontier or frontier temples.
  • 趺坐 - A term for the lotus position used in meditation, common in both Daoist and Buddhist traditions.
  • 功益精进 - A phrase indicating spiritual progress, used in Buddhist and Daoist contexts to describe deepening meditation and cultivation.

Proper Nouns

  • 燕山 - Yanshan Mountains; a geographical location in northern China.

  • 車轟雷吼 - Vivid metaphor for a loud, thunderous sound, likely describing the noise of a vehicle.
  • 山谷振動 - Mountains and valleys trembled; emphasizes the intensity of the sound.

  • 無所遇而還 - Found nothing and returned; indicates a lack of significant spiritual discoveries.
  • 游終南山 - Visited Mount Zhongnan; indicates another pilgrimage.
  • 遍拜諸寺 - Visited all the temples; indicates a thorough spiritual journey.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大蟒 - Large python; in Chinese culture, can symbolize danger or supernatural forces.
  • 先覺 - Wise or enlightened person; indicates a spiritual or philosophical figure.

  • 嘉峪關 - The Jiayu Pass; a historically significant border pass in China.

Proper Nouns

  • 嘉峪關 - The Jiayu Pass.
  • 西域 - The Western Regions.
  • 終南山 - Mount Zhongnan.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 佥云 - Term denoting a collective statement or rumor, often translated as 'it is said' or 'it is rumored.'
  • 山之阳，其山阴 - Refers to the north and south sides of a mountain, respectively. '阳' (yang) means the sunny side, and '阴' (yin) means the shady side.
  • 相隔黄流 - Literally 'separated by the yellow stream,' referring to the Yellow River, a significant geographical feature in Chinese history.
  • 树木阴森 - Describes trees as dense and dark, often implying an eerie or foreboding atmosphere.
  • 无敢入者 - Means 'no one dares to enter,' indicating the fear associated with the place.
  • 即有好事之人，去亦不返 - Refers to those who are curious or adventurous but do not return, suggesting a dangerous or mysterious place.
  • 相传为栖真之所 - Means 'it is said to be a place where true nature or spiritual beings reside,' indicating a sacred or mystical location.
  • 先觉欣然愿往 - '先觉' (xianjue) refers to someone who has already attained enlightenment or insight. '欣然愿往' means 'eagerly wishes to go.'
  • 或阻之，不听 - '或阻之' means 'some try to stop him,' and '不听' means 'he does not listen,' indicating determination.
  • 携带干粮 - Means 'carrying dry food,' referring to provisions for a journey.
  • 渡河而入 - Means 'crossing the river and entering,' describing the physical act of crossing the Yellow River.
  • 参天覆地 - Describes trees as towering and widespread, emphasizing their grandeur.
  • 枝叶相接 - Means 'branches and leaves interconnect,' indicating a dense forest canopy.
  • 朦朦胧胧 - Means 'dim and indistinct,' describing a hazy or unclear environment.
  • 先觉由之 - Means 'the enlightened one proceeds,' indicating the subject's continued journey.
  • 日则攀葛结藤，夜则调息入定 - '日则' means 'during the day,' and '夜则' means 'at night.' '攀葛结藤' means 'climbing vines and tying them together,' and '调息入定' means 'regulating breath and entering a meditative state.'
  • 约行七八日 - Means 'after about seven or eight days,' indicating the duration of the journey.
  • 始出树林 - Means 'finally emerges from the forest,' marking a transition from the dense woods.
  • 现奇峰叠障 - Describes unusual and layered mountains, emphasizing their beauty and complexity.
  • 千态万状 - Means 'thousands of forms and appearances,' indicating a variety of landscapes.
  • 或以高，或崭而削，或以幽，或崎岖而险 - '或以高' means 'some are high,' '或崭而削' means 'some are sharp and steep,' '或以幽' means 'some are secluded,' and '或崎岖而险' means 'some are rugged and dangerous,' describing the diverse and challenging terrain.
  • 径路盘曲，似有人行者 - '径路盘曲' means 'paths winding and twisting,' and '似有人行者' means 'as if there are people walking,' suggesting the presence of human activity.
  • 复数日 - Means 'after several more days,' indicating the continuation of the journey.
  • 出山坳 - Means 'emerging from a valley,' marking another transition.
  • 势更 - Means 'the scenery changes,' indicating a shift in the landscape.
  • 莫穷莫尽 - Means 'endless and boundless,' describing the vast and unending nature of the scenery.
  • 秀色可餐 - Means 'beautiful enough to eat,' emphasizing the aesthetic appeal of the landscape.
  • 无虎狼恶兽患 - Means 'no danger from tigers, wolves, or other wild beasts,' indicating safety from wildlife.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 佥云 - This term reflects the cultural practice of spreading rumors or collective beliefs, often used to convey a sense of mystery or unverified information.
  • 山之阳，其山阴 - These terms are rooted in Chinese geomancy and feng shui, where the north and south sides of a mountain have different energies and characteristics.
  • 黄流 - Refers to the Yellow River, one of the most significant rivers in Chinese history and culture, often symbolizing the cradle of Chinese civilization.
  • 树木阴森 - This phrase is often used in classical literature to create a sense of foreboding or supernatural presence, reflecting the cultural belief in the power of nature.
  • 栖真之所 - This term is associated with Daoist and Buddhist traditions, where certain places are believed to be inhabited by spiritual beings or used for meditation and enlightenment.
  • 先觉 - This term is often used in Confucian and Daoist texts to describe individuals who have achieved a higher level of understanding or spiritual insight.

Proper Nouns

  • 黄流 - Refers to the Yellow River, a significant geographical feature in Chinese history.
  • 先觉 - Refers to an individual who has achieved enlightenment or spiritual insight.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 时见石龛 - Means 'occasionally seeing stone niches,' interpreted as 'seeing stone niches from time to time.'
  • 隐隐有人趺坐 - '隐隐' means 'faintly' or 'vaguely,' and '趺坐' means 'cross-legged sitting.' This phrase is interpreted as 'vaguely seeing someone sitting cross-legged.'
  • 第四面峭壁 - Literally means 'the fourth side steep cliff,' interpreted as 'the fourth side is a sheer cliff.'
  • 无路可通 - Means 'no path can be found,' interpreted as 'there is no way to pass through.'
  • 不知从何处登也 - Means 'do not know from where to ascend,' interpreted as 'do not know where to climb from.'
  • 先觉呼之不应 - Means 'the awakened one calls out but gets no response,' interpreted as 'the awakened one calls but receives no answer.'

  • 又无水可饮 - Means 'there is also no water to drink,' interpreted as 'there is also no water to drink.'
  • 虽闻潺潺之声 - Means 'although hearing the sound of flowing water,' interpreted as 'although hearing the sound of the stream.'
  • 而溪皆深邃 - Means 'but the streams are all deep and remote,' interpreted as 'but the streams are all deep and secluded.'

  • 迎面一古衣冠道人来 - Means 'a man of antiquity with ancient attire comes face to face,' interpreted as 'a man of antiquity with ancient attire comes face to face.'
  • 先觉欢欣相接 - Means 'the awakened one rejoices to meet,' interpreted as 'the awakened one rejoices to meet.'
  • 牵其衣裾而拜 - Means 'pulling his garment and bowing,' interpreted as 'pulling his garment and bowing.'
  • 予凡夫耳，焉得仙 - Means 'I am just an ordinary man, how can I be an immortal,' interpreted as 'I am just an ordinary man, how can I be an immortal.'
  • 仙人肯使汝见耶 - Means 'would the Immortal allow you to see him,' interpreted as 'would the Immortal allow you to see him.'
  • 相与就石凳共坐 - Means 'together sit on a stone bench,' interpreted as 'together sit on a stone bench.'
  • 叩所从来 - Means 'inquiring about where he comes from,' interpreted as 'inquiring about where he comes from.'
  • 予世居越国 - Means 'I have lived in Yue State,' interpreted as 'I have lived in Yue State.'
  • 徐姓 - Means 'last name Xu,' interpreted as 'last name Xu.'
  • 秦皇时为役夫 - Means 'during the Qin Emperor's time, I was a laborer,' interpreted as 'during the Qin Emperor's time, I was a laborer.'
  • 是楚汉纷争 - Means 'it was during the Chu-Han contention,' interpreted as 'it was during the Chu-Han contention.'
  • 投楚而卒 - Means 'joined Chu and died,' interpreted as 'joined Chu and died.'
  • 乌江之败 - Means 'the defeat at the Black River,' interpreted as 'the defeat at the Black River.'
  • 逃入此山，不复出矣 - Means 'fled into this mountain and never came out again,' interpreted as 'fled into this mountain and never came out again.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 先觉 - Refers to a person who has awakened or realized something profound, often used in spiritual or philosophical contexts.
  • 膜拜 - A form of worship or deep respect, often involving kneeling and prostrating oneself.
  • 大仙 - A term for an immortal or highly revered spiritual figure.
  • 秦皇 - Refers to the First Emperor of Qin, Qin Shi Huang, a significant historical figure.
  • 楚汉纷争 - Refers to the Chu-Han Contention, a period of conflict between the states of Chu and Han during the late Qin dynasty.
  • 乌江之败 - Refers to the Battle of the Black River, a significant battle during the Chu-Han Contention where Xiang Yu, the leader of Chu, was defeated.
  • 越国 - Refers to the ancient state of Yue, located in what is now Zhejiang Province.

Proper Nouns

  • 先觉 - The awakened one.
  • 大仙 - Great Immortal.
  • 秦皇 - Qin Shi Huang.
  • 楚汉纷争 - Chu-Han Contention.
  • 乌江之败 - Battle of the Black River.
  • 越国 - State of Yue.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 先覺 - Term that can mean 'the enlightened one' or 'the one who has awakened,' often used in spiritual or mystical contexts. It may also be a proper name in this context.
  • 渴甚 - Classical expression meaning 'extremely thirsty.'
  • 身解佩匙 - Literal phrase meaning 'unfastened the pendant key' or 'removed the pendant key from his person.' The term *佩匙* may be a specific object, possibly symbolic.
  • 挖之 - 'To dig it,' referring to digging into the stone mountain.
  • 得水与饮 - 'Got water and gave it to drink.'
  • 清凉渗膏 - Poetic or metaphorical expression. *清凉* means 'cooling' or 'refreshing,' *渗膏* may mean 'oily seepage' or 'greasy moisture,' possibly metaphorical.
  • 连给二匙 - 'Gave two spoonfuls in succession.'
  • 收藏欲去 - 'Collected it and wanted to leave.'
  • 弟子尚未足也 - 'The disciple is not yet satisfied,' a formal or respectful way of expressing incomplete fulfillment.
  • 不但汝渴已已 - 'Not only are you no longer thirsty,' *已已* is a classical expression for 'already.'
  • 即不食亦无碍矣 - 'Even if you do not eat, it will not be a problem.'
  • 何必多饮 - 'Why drink so much?'
  • 持裾不释 - 'Held onto the hem of the robe and would not let go.'
  • 哀求超度 - 'Sought to be saved' or 'pleaded for deliverance.'
  • 道不同，不相为谋 - Well-known classical saying meaning 'If the paths are different, one cannot plan together' or 'Those with different ways cannot be partners.'
  • 我非汝师 - 'I am not your teacher.'
  • 汝必欲度 - 'You must wish to be saved.'
  • 不见前山又有人来耶 - 'Do you not see that another person is coming from the front mountain?' *耶* is a question particle.

  • 青蓝布一握 - 'A handful of blue-green cloth.'
  • 玉大士像 - 'A jade statue of the Bodhisattva.'

  • 身体益轻健 - 'Body became even lighter and healthier.'
  • 不食不饥 - 'Did not eat and was not hungry.'
  • 日行四五百里 - 'Traveled four to five hundred miles a day.'
  • 遂出终南 - 'Then left Zhongnan,' referring to the Zhongnan Mountains.
  • 游山左 - 'Traveled to the left of the mountains' or 'traveled to the eastern regions of the mountain range.'
  • 不复反 - 'Did not return again.'
  • 或曰 - 'Some say,' an introductory phrase for a possible legend or alternative account.
  • 在青莱之间 - 'In the area between Qing and Lai,' referring to regions in Shandong.
  • 山深不知处耳 - 'The mountains are deep, so one does not know the location,' indicating the difficulty of locating the place.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 先觉 - May refer to a spiritual teacher or enlightened being, possibly a historical or legendary figure.
  • 玉大士像 - A jade statue of a Bodhisattva, likely referring to Guanyin or another Buddhist figure. This reflects the influence of Buddhism in Chinese culture.
  • 石髓 - A term found in Daoist and alchemical texts, referring to a substance believed to be formed from stone, often associated with longevity or spiritual transformation.
  • 山左 - Refers to the area to the east of the mountains, traditionally used to describe the eastern part of China, particularly Shandong.
  • 青莱之间 - Refers to the region between Qingdao (青) and Laiyang (莱), in Shandong province.
  • 不复反 - Reflects a common motif in Daoist and Buddhist texts, where a disciple or sage leaves the world behind to pursue spiritual enlightenment.

Proper Nouns

  • 先觉 - A title or name, possibly a spiritual figure or a specific person in the legend. Rendered as 'The Enlightened One' or 'The Awakened One' unless a specific name is known.
  • 玉大士像 - 'The Jade Bodhisattva Statue' or 'The Jade Statue of the Bodhisattva.'
  • 青莱之间 - A geographical region, likely referring to areas near Qingdao and Laiyang in Shandong province.

[443]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 幼名多官 - A phrase indicating that the person was named 'Duo Guan' when he was young.
  • 溺爱过甚 - Literally means 'overwhelming affection,' referring to excessive parental love.
  • 听其所为 - Means 'to allow him to do as he pleases.'
  • 读书不就 - Indicates that the person did not succeed in his studies.
  • 不事别业 - Means 'not engaged in any other profession.'
  • 鸣钲开道 - Refers to the sound of a bell being rung to clear the way, often associated with officials or dignitaries.
  • 升堂理事 - Means 'to ascend the hall to handle affairs,' a metaphor for taking charge and making decisions.
  • 詈人挞人 - Refers to scolding and beating others, actions taken by officials to enforce authority.
  • 皆不敢违拗 - Means 'nobody dares to disobey.'
  • 心甚艳之 - Indicates that the person greatly admired these actions.
  • 演习 - Means 'to practice' or 'to imitate.'
  • 疯子 - Literally means 'madman,' but in this context, it refers to someone who behaves strangely or is considered eccentric.
  • 逢人则问官何来 - Means 'he would ask everyone where the official came from.'
  • 诳之者 - Refers to someone who deceives or lies to him.
  • 官易事耳 - Means 'being an official is easy.'
  • 但须北京去见皇帝，求之即得 - Means 'but one must go to Beijing to see the emperor and request it.'
  • 窃其父金数两 - Means 'he stole several taels of gold from his father.'
  • 往北逸去 - Means 'he fled north.'
  • 至浦口，一无所有矣 - Means 'he arrived at Pu Kou and had nothing left.'
  • 官舫之赴楚者 - Refers to an official boat heading to Chu (a region in ancient China).
  • 觅纤手 - Means 'to seek out a person to pull the boat.'
  • 昧然应募 - Means 'he blindly volunteered.'
  • 日行百里 - Means 'he traveled a hundred li (about 50 kilometers) each day.'
  • 众皆知其疯 - Means 'everyone knew he was crazy.'
  • 皆玩戏之 - Means 'everyone treated him as a joke.'
  • 惟官舫中一叟 - Refers to an elderly man on the official boat.
  • 屡目顾之 - Means 'he often looked at him.'
  • 周其衣食 - Means 'he took care of his clothing and food.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 江右 - Refers to the western part of the Yangtze River, specifically the region that includes modern-day Jiangxi province.
  • 华三祝 - The name of the protagonist, indicating he is from the Hu family and his full name is 'San Zhu.'
  • 幼名多官 - The name 'Duo Guan' is a childhood name, which is common in traditional Chinese naming conventions.
  • 鸣钲开道 - A practice in ancient China where officials would have their way cleared by ringing a bell, symbolizing their authority.
  • 升堂理事 - A metaphor for an official taking charge and making decisions, often in a formal setting.
  • 詈人挞人 - Actions typically performed by officials to enforce discipline and maintain order.
  • 北京 - Refers to the capital city, which was the political center of the empire.
  • 浦口 - A place name, likely referring to a port or riverbank area.
  • 楚 - An ancient state in southern China, often used in historical and literary contexts.

Proper Nouns

  • 华三祝 - The protagonist's full name.
  • 江右 - The region where the protagonist is from.
  • 北京 - The capital city.
  • 浦口 - A specific location.
  • 楚 - An ancient state.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一日 - Temporal marker meaning 'one day' or 'at one time,' setting the scene.
  • 遇飓风 - Means 'encountered a typhoon,' a natural disaster in the narrative.
  • 停泊河畔 - Means 'anchored by the riverbank,' describing the setting.

  • The phrase '大而无当，将必改为小用' is a logical conclusion, which must be read as a single thought.
  • The phrase '朱轮华毂，王果至庄' is a descriptive statement, which must be read as a single event.
  • The phrase '先索定契银三千两，余俟折换之日准算' is a financial statement, which must be read as a single condition.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '匠告以故，门官入白王' is compact, with the craftsman informing someone of a reason, and the gatekeeper reporting to the king.
  • The craftsman looks at the king's appearance and realizes it is not the same person as before.

  • The shopkeeper asks what is needed, and the person requests 500 strings of cash, but the shopkeeper reduces it to 300 strings.
  • The northern region uses paper money, and the person is given the equivalent amount and leaves.
  • A beggar arrives and pawns his torn coat for 20 strings of cash.
  • The shopkeeper becomes suspicious and re-examines the bracelets, discovering they have been replaced with fake gold.
  • The shopkeeper asks the beggar how he knew, and the beggar explains that fake gold bracelets are worth 300 strings, while his coat, though torn, is not fake.
  • The beggar knows the swindler's residence, implying he is aware of the deception.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 掌柜人 - A term for a shopkeeper or innkeeper, likely referring to the owner of a business or lodging house.
  • 丐 - A beggar or mendicant; in this context, a person who is poor and may be involved in the incident.
  • 两贯 - A unit of currency; in classical Chinese, '贯' is a string of coins, and two '贯' would be 200 coins, depending on the era.
  • 偕往寻之 - 'Go together to find him'; a compound phrase expressing joint pursuit.
  • 得钱脱身去矣 - 'Got the money and fled'; a common phrase in narrative texts to indicate escape after receiving payment.
  • 未敢喧哗 - 'Did not dare to make a commotion'; reflects social hierarchy and restraint in the presence of someone of higher status.
  • 寓主通其仆 - 'The innkeeper communicated with his servant'; '通' here means to communicate or send a message.
  • 与之辩论 - 'Argued with him'; a common phrase in classical Chinese for disputing or debating.
  • 物既伪，何以质钱如此之多 - 'If the item is fake, why would you need so much money as a deposit?' A rhetorical question used to challenge the legitimacy of the transaction.
  • 明是汝换我也 - 'Clearly you have switched it on me'; a direct accusation of deception.
  • 互相争执 - 'They quarreled with each other'; a standard expression for conflict.
  • 显者闻声邀二人入 - 'The prominent person heard the commotion and invited the two in'; '显者' is a term for a person of high social or official rank.
  • 宁吃亏，勿占便宜 - 'Rather suffer a loss than take advantage'; a common moral maxim.
  • 不可与市井之徒较量，有失官礼 - 'It is not proper to argue with commoners; it would be unbecoming of an official'; reflects Confucian ideals of decorum and hierarchy.
  • 委屈听命 - 'Reluctantly obeyed'; indicates unwillingness but submission to authority.
  • 原钱帖赎还二镯 - 'Returned the two bracelets using the original money note'; '帖' here likely refers to a receipt or voucher.
  • 欣然领去 - 'Joyfully accepted'; a phrase indicating satisfaction with the resolution.
  • 至晚至钱局取钱 - 'By evening, he went to the money office to collect the money'; '钱局' is likely an official money office or mint.
  • 系好手描摹者 - 'It was a well-crafted imitation by hand'; '描摹' means to copy or imitate, and '好手' refers to a skilled hand or artisan.
  • 丐亦不知所往 - 'The beggar also had disappeared'; a common narrative closure in classical Chinese.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 掌柜人 - A term used in classical and traditional Chinese society to refer to the owner or manager of a shop, inn, or business. This reflects the commercial and social structure of pre-modern China.
  • 丐 - A term for a beggar or a person living in poverty. In classical Chinese narratives, beggars often appear as peripheral figures or as intermediaries in transactions.
  • 两贯 - A unit of currency. In classical China, a '贯' was a string of 100 copper coins, so two '贯' would be 200 coins. The exact value of this in modern currency is uncertain, but it is sufficient to indicate a significant sum.
  • 寓 - A term for a lodging house or inn. This reflects the social structure of travelers and the existence of commercial inns in ancient China.
  • 显者 - A term for a person of high social or official rank, likely a scholar-official or noble. This term reflects the Confucian value system, in which social hierarchy and proper conduct are emphasized.
  • 钱局 - Likely refers to an official government office responsible for minting or exchanging money. This reflects the bureaucratic and economic systems of ancient China.
  • 原钱帖 - A term that likely refers to a receipt or voucher for money. This reflects the use of written documentation in commercial transactions.
  • 市井之徒 - A term for common people or people from the marketplace, often used in a derogatory sense in classical Chinese literature to distinguish between the elite and the common people.

  • 报琼 - Metaphor from ancient Chinese literature where returning a valuable gift, such as jade, symbolizes a gesture of gratitude or reciprocity.
  • 学使 - Examiner or supervisor of academic examinations during the imperial examination system, crucial in Chinese society.
  • 窗课 - Lessons or studies, often associated with private tutoring or self-study, important for preparing for the imperial examinations.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '施等皆下樓，謂金曰' introduces the action of Shi and others going downstairs and speaking to Jin. The sentence structure is straightforward, but the use of '等' (děng) and '皆' (jiē) emphasizes the collective nature of the action.
  • The phrase '三生有幸，始得定交' uses a concessive structure to emphasize the rarity of the opportunity to establish a friendship.
  • The phrase '如蒙相思，只須告把門張老，我自来耳' is a conditional statement that outlines the steps Shi would take if Jin were to think of him.
  • The phrase '作别乘舆乘马，纷纷而散' describes the departure of the group in a scattered manner, using parallel structure to convey the idea of a disorganized departure.
  • The phrase '佥参赞作报琼之举，以申敬意' uses a compound predicate to describe the collective action of the group in expressing their gratitude.
  • The phrase '遂于旅邸盛设，具柬交张老以邀三君子' describes the preparation of a grand feast at the inn and the delivery of a formal invitation to Old Zhang to invite the three gentlemen.

  • 蒋司马 - Refers to Jiang Sima, likely a high-ranking official.
  • 省垣 - Refers to the provincial capital or administrative center.
  • 太守 - Refers to the prefect, a high-ranking local official.
  • 柑桔 - Refers to tangerines, a common fruit in China.
  • 骗子手 - Refers to a thief or pickpocket.
  • 河浜 - Refers to a riverbank or small stream.

Proper Nouns

  • 蒋司马 - Jiang Sima, a high-ranking official.
  • 省垣 - The provincial capital or administrative center.
  • 太守 - The prefect, a high-ranking local official.
  • 河浜 - A riverbank or small stream.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 其人即跃上 - Direct translation of someone jumping onto a boat.
  • 舟中亦有如是一卷行李 - Descriptive phrase indicating the presence of similar luggage on the boat.
  • 背负之 - Literal translation of carrying on one's back.
  • 登岸竟去 - Direct translation of going ashore and leaving.
  • 自弃其米 - Literal translation of abandoning one's rice.
  • 为人负重妆点 - Literal translation of being burdened by someone else and decorated.
  • 何为耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'why is this?'
  • 此人何其呆也 - Literal translation of how foolish this person is.
  • 一去不顾 - Direct translation of leaving without looking back.
  • 恐是骗子 - Literal translation of probably a fraud.
  • 悟急追 - Direct translation of realizing and chasing urgently.
  • 不知所之矣 - Literal translation of no longer knowing where he went.
  • 儒生赴岁试者 - Literal translation of a scholar going to take the annual examination.
  • 行李迁起 - Direct translation of the baggage being moved.
  • 尚未议定脚价 - Literal translation of the price for transportation not being agreed upon.
  • 姑坐行李上守之 - Direct translation of temporarily sitting on the baggage to guard it.
  • 长揖 - Direct translation of performing a formal bow.
  • 兄台何自来耶 - Literal translation of how did you come here, sir?
  • 细认，彼此皆不识 - Literal translation of looking carefully, but neither recognizing the other.
  • 误矣 - Literal translation of mistaken.
  • 揖谢不安而别 - Literal translation of bowing and leaving with discomfort.
  • 仰后倒地 - Direct translation of falling backward.
  • 方知行李已为人抽去 - Literal translation of only then realizing that the baggage has been taken away.
  • 龙有嗜，可豢之 - Literal translation of a dragon has a taste, it can be tamed.
  • 物先腐，虫生之 - Literal translation of if an object is already rotten, worms will grow.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 岁试 - Refers to the annual examination taken by scholars in ancient China.
  • 儒生 - Refers to a scholar or a person who studies Confucianism.
  • 长揖 - A formal bow used in ancient China, often in a respectful or polite context.
  • 兄台 - A respectful term for 'sir' or 'mister' in ancient Chinese.
  • 龙 - In Chinese culture, dragons are often symbols of power, strength, and good fortune.
  • 物先腐，虫生之 - A proverb that suggests if something is already in a state of decay, it will attract more decay or corruption.

Proper Nouns

  • 岁试 - The annual examination.
  • 儒生 - The scholar.
  • 长揖 - The formal bow.
  • 兄台 - The respectful term for 'sir.'
  • 龙 - The dragon.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 诸公皆有隙可乘 - Literally 'all the great men have gaps that can be exploited,' meaning that all prominent figures have weaknesses or opportunities for manipulation.
  • 入其玄中 - Literally 'enter into their profound realm,' suggesting gaining access to their inner thoughts or secrets.
  • 回视人间世，了无一事真 - A quote attributed to Su Shi (Su Dongpo), meaning 'looking back at the human world, there is nothing truly real,' reflecting a philosophical detachment from worldly affairs.
  • 百货杂伪 - Literally 'a hundred goods mixed with falsehoods,' referring to the prevalence of fake or inferior goods in the market.
  • 医者充市，畴为张长沙刘河间耶 - Literally 'doctors fill the market, who then are Zhang Changsha and Liu Hejian?' Zhang Changsha and Liu Hejian were famous physicians, and the phrase questions the authenticity of modern doctors.
  • 星者盈街，畴为李虚中袁天罡耶 - Literally 'astrologers fill the streets, who then are Li Xuzhong and Yuan Tiangang?' Li Xuzhong and Yuan Tiangang were renowned astrologers, and the phrase questions the authenticity of modern astrologers.
  • 名为习申韩者，不知申韩是一是二，是何代人，何曾读不害非子之书耶 - Literally 'those who claim to study Shang Yang and Han Fei, do not know whether Shang and Han are one or two, of what era they are, or whether they have ever read the books of not harming or not being wrong.' This criticizes those who claim to study the Legalist philosophers Shang Yang and Han Fei but lack true understanding.
  • 九流杂技 - Literally 'nine schools of mixed skills,' referring to the various schools of thought and skills in Chinese philosophy and culture.
  • 科甲遍宇内 - Literally 'imperial examinations spread throughout the world,' referring to the widespread practice of the imperial examination system.
  • 班马屈宋之词华 - Literally 'the eloquence of Ban Gu, Sima Qian, Qu Yuan, and Song Yu,' referring to the literary brilliance of these four famous writers.
  • 周程张朱之理学 - Literally 'the rationalist philosophy of Zhou Dunyi, Cheng Hao, Zhang Zai, and Zhu Xi,' referring to the Neo-Confucian scholars.
  • 八股时文 - Literally 'eight-part essays of the time,' referring to the standardized essays required in the imperial examinations.
  • 骗功名耳 - Literally 'just to cheat for fame and rank,' criticizing the superficial pursuit of success.
  • 纷纷一大世界 - Literally 'a world full of confusion,' describing a chaotic and complex world.
  • 人骗己，己骗人，有时己亦骗己 - Literally 'people deceive others, others deceive people, sometimes one even deceives oneself,' criticizing the pervasive nature of deceit.
  • 骗子手 - Literally 'cheaters,' referring to those who deceive others.
  • 一片婆心 - Literally 'a heart full of compassion,' referring to a genuine and caring attitude.
  • 唤醒世间多少自欺欺人之辈 - Literally 'awaken the many who deceive themselves and others,' criticizing those who are self-deceived and deceive others.
  • 笔亦古秀而健 - Literally 'the writing is also ancient, elegant, and robust,' praising the style of the writing.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 诸公 - Refers to prominent or influential people.
  • 张长沙刘河间 - Zhang Changsha and Liu Hejian were famous physicians of the Han Dynasty, known for their medical expertise.
  • 李虚中袁天罡 - Li Xuzhong and Yuan Tiangang were renowned astrologers of the Tang Dynasty, known for their divination skills.
  • 申韩 - Refers to Shang Yang and Han Fei, two prominent Legalist philosophers of the Warring States period.
  • 班马屈宋 - Refers to Ban Gu, Sima Qian, Qu Yuan, and Song Yu, four famous writers and scholars of the Han and Warring States periods.
  • 周程张朱 - Refers to Zhou Dunyi, Cheng Hao, Zhang Zai, and Zhu Xi, four prominent Neo-Confucian scholars of the Song Dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 张长沙刘河间 - Zhang Changsha and Liu Hejian, famous physicians of the Han Dynasty.
  • 李虚中袁天罡 - Li Xuzhong and Yuan Tiangang, renowned astrologers of the Tang Dynasty.
  • 申韩 - Shang Yang and Han Fei, Legalist philosophers of the Warring States period.
  • 班马屈宋 - Ban Gu, Sima Qian, Qu Yuan, and Song Yu, famous writers of the Han and Warring States periods.
  • 周程张朱 - Zhou Dunyi, Cheng Hao, Zhang Zai, and Zhu Xi, Neo-Confucian scholars of the Song Dynasty.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 某学广文 - Formal expression meaning 'a certain scholar of the Guangwen school,' referring to a type of education or academic institution.
  • 耄而贪 - Means 'old and greedy,' describing a person who is both advanced in age and prone to greed.
  • 诸生皆恶之 - Means 'all the students hated him,' indicating the widespread dislike of the scholar.
  • 适有少年科甲之学使来 - Means 'just then, a young scholar from the imperial examination arrived,' referring to a newly appointed official.
  • 最恶白髯 - Means 'most hated white beards,' indicating a strong aversion to older individuals.
  • 见之则曰 - Means 'when he saw him, he said,' introducing a direct speech.
  • 汝已老大，好让后生矣 - Means 'you are already old, it's time to give way to the younger generation,' a dismissive statement.
  • 必罢之 - Means 'he must dismiss him,' indicating the official's intention to remove the scholar.
  • 故斑白者皆闻声而惧 - Means 'therefore, all the gray-haired ones heard and were afraid,' describing the reaction of older individuals.
  • 皆广文须发浩然 - Means 'all the Guangwen scholars had abundant beards and hair,' describing their appearance.
  • 遍求乌药 - Means 'searched everywhere for black medicine,' referring to a remedy or treatment.
  • 又不肯解囊 - Means 'but he was unwilling to open his purse,' indicating reluctance to spend money.
  • 勒派诸生代觅 - Means 'forced the students to find it on his behalf,' describing the scholar's demand.
  • 有生谓之曰 - Means 'a student told him,' introducing another direct speech.
  • 门生之戚，宦于东粤 - Means 'a relative of my student, who serves in the eastern part of Guangdong,' providing background information.
  • 有好乌药，名透骨丹 - Means 'has good black medicine, called Tougudan,' describing the remedy.
  • 初染色红，三复之，则黑如明漆 - Means 'at first it turns red, after three repetitions, it becomes black like polished lacquer,' describing the process of dyeing.
  • 泽润有光 - Means 'lustrous and shiny,' describing the appearance of the dyed hair.
  • 真无价之宝也 - Means 'truly a priceless treasure,' emphasizing the value of the remedy.
  • 门生感师恩 - Means 'my student is grateful for the teacher's kindness,' expressing gratitude.
  • 仅分得少许 - Means 'only obtained a small amount,' indicating the limited quantity.
  • 敬以奉赠 - Means 'respectfully offers it as a gift,' describing the act of giving.
  • 广文大悦，谢而受之 - Means 'the Guangwen scholar was very pleased, thanked him and accepted it,' describing the scholar's reaction.
  • 如法试验 - Means 'tried it according to the method,' describing the process of testing.
  • 一染而红，再染而绛，三染而紫赤色 - Means 'the first dye turned red, the second turned dark red, the third turned purple and red,' describing the results of the dyeing process.
  • 愈洗则愈鲜明 - Means 'the more it was washed, the more vivid it became,' describing the effect of washing.
  • 俨如道院中所塑之祝融像 - Means 'it looked like the statue of Zhurong in a Taoist temple,' comparing the appearance to a religious figure.
  • 见者大笑 - Means 'those who saw it laughed,' describing the reaction of others.
  • 寻其门人，不知所往 - Means 'searching for his students, he did not know where they went,' indicating the disappearance of the students.
  • 竟不敢赴试 - Means 'he finally dared not take the exam,' describing the scholar's decision.
  • 致仕归去 - Means 'retired and returned home,' describing the scholar's retirement.
  • 生始告人 - Means 'the student then told others,' introducing a revelation.
  • 其药以龟溺熬紫草为之 - Means 'the medicine was made by boiling purple grass with turtle urine,' describing the composition of the remedy.
  • 即染鬃缨之法 - Means 'it was the method of dyeing horsehair,' providing a comparison.
  • 岂能改色乎 - Means 'how could it change color?' expressing skepticism.
  • 此广文者，俾终生为红胡子矣 - Means 'this Guangwen scholar will be a red-bearded man for life,' describing the final outcome.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 广文 - Refers to a type of education or academic institution, possibly a school or a specific educational system.
  • 耄而贪 - A common description of an old and greedy person, reflecting a negative stereotype.
  • 少年科甲之学使 - Refers to a young scholar who has passed the imperial examinations and been appointed to an official position.
  • 白髯 - Symbolizes old age and wisdom, but in this context, it is associated with a negative reaction.
  • 斑白者 - Refers to older individuals, often associated with wisdom and experience.
  • 乌药 - Refers to a black medicine or remedy, possibly a dye or treatment.
  • 透骨丹 - A specific name for a black medicine, possibly a dye or treatment.
  • 门生之戚 - Refers to a relative of a student, indicating a connection through education.
  • 东粤 - Refers to the eastern part of Guangdong, a region in southern China.
  • 祝融 - A mythological figure in Chinese culture, often associated with fire and the south.
  • 道院 - Refers to a Taoist temple, a place of religious practice.
  • 鬃缨 - Refers to horsehair, often used in traditional Chinese clothing and accessories.
  • 龟溺 - Refers to turtle urine, a component of the remedy.
  • 紫草 - Refers to purple grass, another component of the remedy.
  • 致仕 - Refers to retirement from public office, a common practice in traditional Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 广文 - A type of education or academic institution.
  • 少年科甲之学使 - A young scholar who has passed the imperial examinations and been appointed to an official position.
  • 白髯 - Symbolizes old age and wisdom.
  • 斑白者 - Older individuals.
  • 乌药 - A black medicine or remedy.
  • 透骨丹 - A specific name for a black medicine.
  • 门生之戚 - A relative of a student.
  • 东粤 - The eastern part of Guangdong.
  • 祝融 - A mythological figure in Chinese culture.
  • 道院 - A Taoist temple.
  • 鬃缨 - Horsehair.
  • 龟溺 - Turtle urine.
  • 紫草 - Purple grass.
