Idiomatic Expressions

  • 三官救劫 - Title or heading referring to the 'Three Officials' (天官、地官、水官) in Daoist cosmology, who govern heaven, earth, and water and are invoked to save people from calamity. '救劫' means 'to rescue from a great disaster' or 'to avert calamity.'
  • 年逾六旬 - Literally 'over sixty years old,' a common way to express age in classical Chinese.
  • 职司田庄 - 'In charge of the estate' or 'responsible for the farm.'
  • 就岸滩伏河面吸之 - 'Crouched by the riverbank and drank from the surface,' a vivid description of a man drinking from a river.
  • 朱书 - 'Red script' or 'written in red ink,' often used in religious or official contexts.
  • 饬雷霆诛击恶犯一名某 - A command written in red ink: 'Order the thunder and lightning to strike and punish one evil criminal named [name].' This is a formulaic Daoist or religious incantation.
  • 老奴自幼无欺心事 - 'This old servant has never committed any deceitful or dishonest deed since childhood.'
  • 持经修善 - 'Studied scriptures and practiced virtue.'
  • 更无为非 - 'Has not done anything wrong.'
  • 其为前世事耶 - 'Is this a matter from a previous life?'
  • 天命不可逃也 - 'Heaven’s decree cannot be escaped.'
  • 检点收藏 - 'To check and store' or 'to organize and secure.'
  • 二子不可信托 - 'The two sons cannot be trusted.'
  • 命其以柩来收殓奴尸 - 'Order them to bring the coffin to bury the servant’s body.'
  • 奴死此树下矣 - 'The servant will die under this tree.'
  • 不敢入城惊众也 - 'I dare not enter the city and alarm the people.'
  • 恋恋不忍 - 'Full of reluctance and unwillingness.'
  • 疾风暴云旋起 - 'A sudden gust of wind and storm clouds arose.'
  • 不得不奔 - 'Could not help but run.'
  • 闻轰击之声甚厉 - 'Hear the sound of a loud, violent strike.'
  • 立唤仆之妻子 - 'Immediately called the servant’s wife and children.'
  • 告以故 - 'Told them the reason.'
  • 皆号泣 - 'All wept and cried.'
  • 欲往护之 - 'Wanted to go protect him.'
  • 其主不允 - 'The master did not allow it.'
  • 未几晴霁 - 'Not long after, the weather cleared up.'
  • 欣然归 - 'Returned joyfully.'
  • 方讶其主之言谬妄 - 'Only then did they realize their master’s words were nonsense.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三官 - The Three Officials (天官、地官、水官), a concept in Daoist cosmology and ritual. They are associated with Heaven, Earth, and Water, and are believed to have the power to grant blessings, forgive sins, and avert disasters.
  • 救劫 - A term from Daoist and folk religious practices referring to the act of rescuing people from great calamities, often through rituals or divine intervention.
  • 持经修善 - A Buddhist or Daoist expression meaning to study scriptures and cultivate virtue.
  • 朱书 - Writing in red ink, a common feature in Daoist, Buddhist, and Confucian ritual texts, often used to denote sacred or official decrees.
  • 饬雷霆诛击恶犯 - A formulaic incantation used in Daoist rituals or folk religious practices, invoking divine forces (thunder and lightning) to punish evil.
  • 柩 - A coffin, often used in funerary contexts.
  • 收殓 - To collect and bury the body, a formal term used in funerary rites.
  • 树下 - 'Under the tree,' a common location in Chinese folklore for supernatural or miraculous events.

Proper Nouns

  • 三官 - The Three Officials (天官、地官、水官), a Daoist concept.
  • 吾邑陈氏 - 'A family surnamed Chen in my hometown' – '吾邑' is a first-person reference to the speaker’s hometown, and '陈氏' is the surname of the family.
  • 仆 - 'Servant' – a common term for a household servant.
  • 其主 - 'His master' – the master of the servant.
  • 老奴 - 'This old servant' – a humble self-reference by the servant.
  • 二子 - 'Two sons' – the servant’s children.
  • 柩 - 'Coffin' – a formal term.
  • 树下 - 'Under the tree' – a common location in folktales for supernatural events.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 瞑目受死 - Literal meaning 'close one's eyes to accept death,' an idiom expressing resignation or acceptance of fate.
  • 虔诚祀之 - Worship it with sincerity, emphasizing devoutness.
  • 扶灾危而救苦难 - To help those in disaster and save those in suffering, a moral statement about divine intervention.
  • 神之功德大矣 - The virtue of the god is great indeed, a statement of divine merit.
  • 福善祸淫 - Reward virtue and punish immorality, a principle of divine justice.
  • 彼苍之定理也 - The fixed law of Heaven, emphasizing divine justice as immutable.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 三官菩萨 - Refers to the Taoist deities of Heaven, Earth, and Water, often worshipped in Chinese folk religion.
  • 《三官经》 - A Taoist scripture associated with the Three Officials, emphasizing repentance and moral conduct.
  • 雷神 - The Thunder God, a divine figure associated with lightning and storms in Chinese mythology.
  • 道士 - A Taoist priest or practitioner, often associated with rituals and supernatural powers.
  • 天君 - A term for a divine or heavenly ruler, often used in Taoist or folk religious contexts.
  • 宪司 - The Censorate, an ancient Chinese governmental body responsible for moral oversight and punishment.
  • 缇骑 - Palace guards or imperial attendants, often associated with enforcement of imperial will.
  • 彼苍 - A poetic term for Heaven, often used in classical Chinese texts to denote divine authority.

Proper Nouns

  • 三官菩萨 - The Three Officials, Taoist deities.
  • 《三官经》 - A Taoist scripture.
  • 雷神 - The Thunder God.
  • 道士 - A Taoist priest.
  • 天君 - A divine or heavenly ruler.
  • 宪司 - The Censorate.
  • 缇骑 - Palace guards.
  • 彼苍 - Heaven.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 倒書 - Written upside down.
  • 秀健如趙文敏筆法 - Elegant and vigorous handwriting, similar to the style of Zhao Mengfu.
  • 入磚三分 - Penetrated the brick by three parts, indicating a deep impression.
  • 石灰補之 - Filled in with lime, a common method of repair.
  • 數日復現 - Appeared again after several days.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《筆談》 - Refers to *Dream Pool Essays* (夢溪筆談) by Shen Kuo (沈括).
  • 《耳談》 - Refers to *Tales Heard* (耳談), a collection of anecdotes and stories.
  • 宋治平中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Renzong of Song during the Zhiping era (1064-1067).
  • 天王寺 - Refers to a temple, likely a significant religious site.
  • 姑蘇 - Refers to Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province known for its cultural and historical significance.
  • 安國寺 - Refers to a temple, likely a significant religious site.
  • 趙文敏 - Refers to Zhao Mengfu (趙孟頫), a famous calligrapher and painter of the Yuan dynasty.

Proper Nouns

  • 《筆談》 - Dream Pool Essays (夢溪筆談)
  • 《耳談》 - Tales Heard (耳談)
  • 宋治平中 - Zhiping era of the Song dynasty
  • 天王寺 - Tianwang Temple
  • 姑蘇 - Suzhou
  • 安國寺 - An Guo Temple
  • 趙文敏 - Zhao Mengfu (趙孟頫)

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吾戚金氏楼居 - Refers to the speaker's relative, the Gold family, who lives in a tower.
  • 炎暑之际 - Means 'during the hot summer.'
  • 以幼孩卧床上，已酣 - Describes a young child sleeping soundly on the bed.
  • 其父母皆下楼去 - Indicates that the parents left the tower.
  • 大雨如注，霹雳一声，穿楼而过 - Describes a heavy rainstorm with thunder, where a bolt of lightning passed through the tower.
  • 在室之人，莫不惊悸丧神 - Means that everyone in the room was terrified and lost their composure.
  • 其父母忆及幼孩在床，谅必惊毙矣 - The parents thought that the child must have been killed by the lightning.
  • 登楼视之，前后窗俱辟，帐亦高卷，不见幼孩 - The parents went back upstairs to check, finding all windows open and the curtains high, but no sign of the child.
  • 觅之，以席卷横置于床下，取出观之，儿睡犹未醒也 - They found the child under the bed, wrapped in a mat, still asleep.
  • 席面朱书一字，非篆非符，无识之者 - There was a red character written on the mat, neither seal script nor magic symbol, and no one could recognize it.
  • 又郑孝廉，暑月赴乡，遇雨，避于房檐下 - Another story about a scholar named Zheng, who encountered rain during the summer and sought shelter under a roof.
  • 雷声甚厉 - The thunder was very loud.
  • 忽见云中坠一火球，后有数十神将，体为云护，惟足着尖靴，相随电光疾下 - Suddenly, a fireball fell from the clouds, followed by dozens of divine generals, their bodies shrouded in clouds, but their feet wearing pointed boots, descending rapidly with lightning.
  • 大震一声，半里外田间击死一人，背有古篆，不知所云 - A loud explosion killed someone in a field half a mile away, with ancient characters on their back, whose meaning was unknown.
  • 然则雷书恒有之，不足异 - It is said that lightning inscriptions are common and not unusual.
  • 所可异者，自古有方履，无尖靴，神亦从时，其为今之神欤 - What is unusual is that ancient times had flat shoes, not pointed boots. Perhaps these are gods of the present era?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 吾戚金氏楼居 - Refers to the Gold family, likely a wealthy or influential family living in a multi-story building.
  • 炎暑之际 - A common description of the hot summer months in China.
  • 霹雳一声，穿楼而过 - Lightning passing through a building is a dramatic and rare event, often associated with divine intervention.
  • 席面朱书一字，非篆非符，无识之者 - The red character on the mat is mysterious and unidentifiable, possibly a sign of supernatural activity.
  • 郑孝廉 - A scholar who has passed the imperial examinations, indicating a high level of education and social status.
  • 雷声甚厉 - Loud thunder is often associated with storms and can be frightening.
  • 火球，后有数十神将 - Fireballs and divine generals are common in Chinese mythology, often associated with deities or supernatural beings.

Proper Nouns

  • 吾戚金氏 - The Gold family, likely a wealthy or influential family.
  • 郑孝廉 - A scholar who has passed the imperial examinations.
  • 方履 - Flat shoes, reflecting ancient fashion.
  • 尖靴 - Pointed boots, reflecting modern fashion.

[484]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 甲于全省 - Comparative phrase meaning 'the best in the entire province.'
  • 暮春之际，举国若狂 - Poetic expression meaning 'at the beginning of summer, the whole country goes wild,' referring to the frenzy of the festival.
  • 公议敛资 - Means 'to publicly discuss and collect funds,' indicating a communal effort.
  • 置办彩衣，务极鲜艳 - Means 'to prepare brightly colored costumes, striving for the utmost brilliance.'
  • 翻新出奇，争奢斗富 - Means 'to innovate and create something novel, competing in luxury and wealth.'

  • 何二伯若座上客，汝为灶下养耶 - 何 means 'why,' 二伯 means 'second elder brother,' 若 means 'like,' 座上客 means 'guest at the table,' 汝 means 'you,' 为 means 'to be,' 灶下养 means 'to cook for,' and 耶 is a particle used at the end of a question. Together, it means 'Why is your second elder brother treated like a guest at the table while you are treated like someone who cooks for them?'
  • 已属无耻 - 已属 means 'already belongs,' and 无耻 means 'shameless.' Together, it means 'already belongs to being shameless.'

  • 为汝妻者，何颜立于妯娌之间耶 - 为汝妻者 means 'as your wife,' 何颜 means 'what face,' 立 means 'to stand,' 于 means 'in,' 妯娌 means 'sisters-in-law,' and 之间 means 'among.' Together, it means 'As your wife, what face can you show among your sisters-in-law?'
  • 我岂乐为，父命难违耳 - 我岂 means 'I do not,' 乐为 means 'enjoy doing,' 父命 means 'father's command,' 难违 means 'hard to disobey,' and 耳 is a particle used at the end of a sentence. Together, it means 'I do not enjoy doing it, but I cannot disobey my father's command.'
  • 我何敢教汝违父命 - 我何 means 'I how dare,' 教 means 'to teach,' 汝 means 'you,' 违 means 'to disobey,' and 父命 means 'father's command.' Together, it means 'How dare I teach you to disobey your father's command.'
  • 汝若有志，亦得进场 - 汝若 means 'if you,' 有志 means 'have the will,' 亦得 means 'also can,' and 进场 means 'enter the examination hall.' Together, it means 'If you have the will, you can also enter the examination hall.'
  • 我与有荣施矣 - 我与 means 'I with,' 有 means 'have,' 荣施 means 'honor and benefit,' and 矣 is a particle used at the end of a sentence. Together, it means 'I will also have honor and benefit.'
  • 童生焉得与大试 - 童生 means 'a candidate for the imperial examination,' 焉得 means 'how can,' 与 means 'to join,' and 大试 means 'major examination.' Together, it means 'How can a candidate for the imperial examination join the major examination?'
  • 奈我命何 - 奈 means 'what,' 我命 means 'my fate,' and 何 is a particle used at the end of a question. Together, it means 'What can I do about my fate?'
  • 我有金珠在 - 我有 means 'I have,' 金珠 means 'gold and jewels,' and 在 means 'in.' Together, it means 'I have gold and jewels.'
  • 汝以质与丈人 - 汝以 means 'you with,' 质 means 'to pledge,' 与 means 'to give to,' and 丈人 means 'father-in-law.' Together, it means 'You should pledge them to your father-in-law.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 共拥至父前 - Literal: 'gathered and brought to the father's presence.' Indicates a collective action of presenting something to a father.
  • 先索报资 - Literal: 'first requested payment.' Suggests a transactional context.
  • 不与名条阅视 - Literal: 'did not give the name list for review.' Indicates refusal or secrecy.
  • 我大儿合中久矣 - Literal: 'my eldest son has been destined to pass for a long time.' Implies a long-standing expectation.
  • 今发已迟 - Literal: 'now it has come too late.' Suggests disappointment or frustration.
  • 否则误矣 - Literal: 'otherwise it would be a mistake.' Indicates a correction or clarification.
  • 岂有烧火三相公得中举人耶 - Literal: 'how could the third son, who tends the fire, pass the imperial examination?' Rhetorical question expressing disbelief or surprise.
  • 果有之，已属万幸 - Literal: 'if it is indeed so, it is already a great fortune.' Indicates a sense of luck or fortune.
  • 不过副车，好则榜尾耳 - Literal: 'it is at most a secondary vehicle, at best the last on the list.' Suggests a low ranking or secondary status.
  • 请定赏例，自观名条可知也 - Literal: 'please set the reward example, and you can know by looking at the name list.' Indicates a request for clarity on rewards.
  • 副举十金，正榜倍之 - Literal: 'a secondary pass is ten gold, the main list is double.' Specifies the reward structure.
  • 元魁如何 - Literal: 'what about the top scorer?' Indicates a question about the highest ranking.
  • 魁则五十，元可百金 - Literal: 'the top scorer is fifty, the first place is a hundred gold.' Specifies the rewards for top positions.
  • 决无此理 - Literal: 'there is absolutely no way.' Indicates strong disbelief or impossibility.
  • 众使书券讫 - Literal: 'the crowd made him write the contract.' Indicates a formal agreement.
  • 攫其百金一纸 - Literal: 'took the hundred gold in one document.' Indicates a sudden or forceful action.
  • 公然第一 - Literal: 'openly the first.' Indicates a clear and public declaration of first place.
  • 骇曰 - Literal: 'shocked and said.' Indicates a reaction of surprise or shock.
  • 文风之变迁 - Literal: 'the change in literary style.' Indicates a shift in cultural or literary trends.
  • 今而后不敢论文矣 - Literal: 'from now on, I dare not discuss literature anymore.' Indicates a decision to stop discussing a topic.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 父前 - Refers to the presence of a father, indicating a familial or hierarchical setting.
  • 报资 - Refers to payment or reward, often associated with transactions or services.
  • 名条 - Refers to a list of names, often used in contexts like examinations or records.
  • 大儿 - Refers to the eldest son, indicating a family hierarchy.
  • 次儿 - Refers to the second son, indicating a family hierarchy.
  • 烧火三相公 - Refers to the third son, often associated with the youngest or least favored son in traditional family structures.
  • 中举人 - Refers to passing the imperial examination, a significant achievement in traditional Chinese society.
  • 副车 - Refers to a secondary or auxiliary vehicle, often used metaphorically.
  • 榜尾 - Refers to the last position on a list, often used in the context of examinations.
  • 元魁 - Refers to the top scorer or first place, indicating the highest ranking.
  • 书券 - Refers to writing a contract or agreement, indicating a formal transaction.
  • 攫 - Refers to taking or seizing something, often with force or suddenness.
  • 文风 - Refers to literary style or trends, indicating cultural or intellectual shifts.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有朱解元者 - There was a Zhu who was a jieyuan.
  • 眇一目 - Blind in one eye.
  • 人呼為朱瞎子 - People called him Zhu the blind.
  • 亦曰朱半仙 - Also known as Zhu the half-immortal.
  • 時文中之能手也 - He was a master of writing at the time.
  • 名噪一時 - His name was famous for a time.
  • 其未發解之前一科 - Before he was selected in the previous examination.
  • 偕友赴試 - He went to take the exam with a friend.
  • 八月初七日 - On the seventh day of the eighth month.
  • 夜夢見二青衣 - He dreamed of two servants in green clothes.
  • 相邀入一殿廷 - They invited him into a palace hall.
  • 有冕旒王者 - There was a king with a crown and streamers.
  • 降階相迎 - He descended from the steps to greet him.
  • 聞先生文名籍甚 - He heard of your great literary reputation.
  • 今有爾浙闱墨 - Now I have your essay from the Zhejiang examination.
  • 請先生為之潤飾 - Please have you polish it.
  • 王者命置筆砚于几而退 - The king ordered the pen and inkstone placed on the table and then withdrew.
  • 朱見元作格法高超 - Zhu saw that the original work was of high quality.
  • 惟稍有未圓融處 - But it was slightly lacking in harmony.
  • 為之易数字 - He changed a few words.
  • 已尽善矣 - It was now perfect.
  • 今科解元 - The current jieyuan.
  • 文才尚好 - The literary talent is still good.
  • 不意该县城隍神来奏 - Unexpectedly, the city god of the county reported.
  • 此生竟有奸人室女事 - This person was involved in a matter of seducing a woman in another's household.
  • 阴德有亏 - There was a deficiency in his virtue.
  • 应削其籍 - His name should be erased from the records.
  • 予已追取下科解元 - I have already retrieved the jieyuan from the next examination.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 質地雖佳，功夫未到 - Literally 'the quality is good, but the skill has not reached,' referring to something of high quality but lacking in refinement or mastery.
  • 何以冠多士 - Means 'why should it be the best among many scholars,' questioning the superiority of something.
  • 且請就文整顿 - Means 'please revise the text,' asking for further refinement.
  • 资格所限，不能易他人也 - Means 'limited by qualifications, one cannot replace another,' indicating a restriction based on credentials.
  • 大加删削，炼作老境笔路 - Means 'heavily revised and polished into a mature writing style,' describing a thorough editing process.
  • 命梦神飞传与之 - Means 'ordered a dream spirit to deliver it,' a metaphorical way of saying something was conveyed through a supernatural or imaginative means.
  • 次第阅竟 - Means 'read through in order,' indicating a thorough review.
  • 先生在后科之元也 - Means 'the teacher will be the top scholar in the next exam,' referring to a future academic success.
  • 拔补下科元缺 - Means 'promoted to fill the top spot in the next exam,' indicating a promotion or recognition.
  • 致所黜之元，系山阴某生奸其邻女 - Means 'the disqualified top scholar was a student from Shan Yin who had seduced a neighbor's daughter,' describing a moral failing.
  • 神目如电，已为所司执秦 - Means 'the divine eye saw clearly, and he was already seized by the authorities,' indicating divine justice.
  • 劝其改过，将来尚可登科也 - Means 'advised him to repent, and he could still pass the exams in the future,' offering a chance for redemption.
  • 至新解元，诚如先生所云 - Means 'as for the new top scholar, indeed as the teacher said,' confirming a prediction.
  • 系新进童子，其父兄皆为词林，仁和人，祖宗功德甚厚 - Means 'a new student, his father and brothers are all scholars, from Ren He, with great ancestral merit,' describing a family background.
  • 子孙科第，未有艾也 - Means 'the descendants' academic achievements will continue indefinitely,' indicating a long-lasting legacy.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然此子拔早一科 - A phrase indicating that the person in question was promoted or advanced prematurely in one category or level.
  • 阴律应减阳寿五年 - A metaphorical expression suggesting that according to some hidden or mystical law, the person’s lifespan would be reduced by five years.
  • 先生亦为之劝勉 - A phrase meaning that the teacher or mentor also encouraged and advised the person.
  • 俾绳其祖武 - A phrase meaning to follow in the footsteps of one's ancestors or predecessors, often implying a high standard or legacy.
  • 不但寿可免减，禄且日增矣 - A phrase indicating that not only would the person avoid a reduction in lifespan, but their fortune or position would also increase daily.
  • 遂命青衣仍送朱回寓 - A phrase meaning that the teacher then ordered a servant (青衣) to take the person (朱) back to their residence.
  • 其仆守之 - A phrase meaning that the person’s servant was watching over them.
  • 见朱忽醒 - A phrase meaning that the servant saw the person suddenly wake up.
  • 何病耶？头场将毕，自误功名，奈何？” - A question asking what illness the person had, noting that the first exam was about to end and they were jeopardizing their chances of success.
  • 遣仆往接其友，录出两元作 - A phrase meaning that the person sent their servant to fetch their friend and copied out two top essays.
  • 俟友回，出门访见山阴生，及仁和童子 - A phrase meaning that after waiting for the friend to return, the person went out to visit another scholar (山阴生) and a young boy (仁和童子).
  • 告以神语，以文为证 - A phrase meaning that the person told them about the divine message and used the copied essays as evidence.
  • 皆惶悚受教 - A phrase meaning that all were deeply moved and accepted the advice.
  • 朱归，视亲友之文，一读破题，即知其中第几名 - A phrase meaning that after returning home, the person read the essays of their friends and relatives and could immediately determine their ranking.
  • 有佳文，曰：“惜哉不售，其伤阴耶？” - A phrase meaning that if there was a good essay, the person would say, 'Too bad it wasn't successful, did it harm their fortune?'
  • 初不之信，后皆不爽 - A phrase meaning that initially, the person did not believe it, but later found that it was always accurate.
  • 遂有半仙之称 - A phrase meaning that the person eventually gained the reputation of being a semi-divine figure.
  • 吾乡有刘君者，应童试不售，去而习申韩业，公然宪幕 - A phrase meaning that in the person’s hometown, there was a man named Liu who failed the children’s exam and then studied the strategies of Shang Yang and Han Fei, eventually becoming a prominent official.
  • 丁卯岁朝，梦迎天榜，伊名列第一 - A phrase meaning that on the first day of the year in the Dingmao year, the man dreamed of welcoming the imperial list and saw his name as first place.
  • 不觉技痒，复理故业，纳监入闱 - A phrase meaning that he couldn’t resist the urge to try again, so he resumed his old studies, entered the examination hall, and took the exam.
  • 头场犯规被贴，居然第一 - A phrase meaning that during the first exam, he made a mistake and was penalized, yet still managed to come in first place.
  • 此鬼神揶揄之耶？抑若吴生之命有天禄耶？ - A phrase meaning that was this a joke from the gods, or did the person have a special destiny like Wu’s?

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 阳寿 - Refers to one’s lifespan or life span.
  • 祖武 - Refers to the footsteps or legacy of one’s ancestors.

  • 仁和童子 - Refers to a young boy from Renhe (a place name).

  • 宪幕 - Refers to a high-ranking official position, often in the context of a legal or administrative role.

  • 吴生 - Refers to a person named Wu, likely a historical or literary figure.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 請姚先生商之 - Please consult Mr. Yao on the matter. A formal request to seek advice.
  • 此事無所再議者 - This matter is beyond further discussion. Indicates that the decision is final or absolute.
  • 不進，則聖怒莫測，難保自家 - If we do not advance, the wrath of the sage (or ruler) is unpredictable, and our own safety is at risk. A conditional statement emphasizing the danger of inaction.
  • 進則雖死猶榮，況生還可必乎矣 - If we advance, even death would be honorable; how much more is survival assured? A rhetorical question emphasizing the honor of action.
  • 制軍思之，躍然而起 - The commander (or general) pondered it, then sprang up. A vivid description of a decisive moment.
  • 先生之言，勝於良藥，吾無病矣 - Mr. Yao’s words are better than medicine; I am now cured. A metaphor for the healing power of wise counsel.
  • 唯有求先生偕渡重洋，始終其事 - I can only ask you to accompany me across the vast ocean and see this matter through to the end. A request for full partnership.
  • 兵貴神速 - Speed is the essence of warfare. A well-known military maxim.
  • 請即啟行 - Please proceed immediately. A formal command.
  • 挽制軍手而出 - He took the general by the hand and led him out. A gesture of support or leadership.
  • 文武官弁，群以升舆 - Civil and military officials all ascended the palanquin. A description of a ceremonial or symbolic act.
  • 至海門，前軍已發，兩翼猶留 - Upon reaching the sea gate, the vanguard had already departed, but the two wings remained. A military formation description.
  • 訊其故，緣艨艟不足 - Inquiring about the reason, it was because the warships were insufficient. A cause-and-effect explanation.
  • 有能以商舟濟我師者，予五品官 - Whoever can transport our troops with merchant ships shall be granted a fifth-rank official position. A reward system.
  • 有舟子來試投之，立給水師守備 - A boatman came to try his luck, and immediately was given the rank of water commander. A swift reward.
  • 群舟爭集，三軍畢登，揚帆沖浪而前 - Many ships gathered, the three armies all boarded, and set sail, braving the waves. A vivid image of a naval mobilization.
  • 海神呵护，一昼夜直抵鹿門 - Protected by the sea god, they reached Luomen in one night. A supernatural or poetic description of a rapid journey.
  • 攻其無備 - They attacked when the enemy was unprepared. A military strategy.
  • 前軍已入，兩翼從之 - The vanguard had already entered, and the two wings followed. A description of a successful invasion.
  • 制軍統全師進圍台城，出逆意外 - The general led the entire army to surround Taicheng, surprising the enemy. A tactical success.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 制軍 - A title for a military commander or general, often used for a regional military governor.
  • 姚先生 - Mr. Yao, likely a scholar or advisor with military or political influence.
  • 聖怒 - The wrath of the sage, a euphemism for the anger of a ruler or emperor.
  • 五品官 - A rank in the Chinese imperial bureaucratic system, corresponding to a mid-level official.
  • 水師守備 - A naval officer rank, likely equivalent to a 'commander of the water forces.'
  • 商舟 - Merchant ships, used here for military transport due to a shortage of warships.

  • 乐甚，以二千金与令，以赏其费 - Literally 'very pleased, give two thousand taels of gold to the magistrate to reward his expenses,' meaning 'very pleased, give two thousand taels of gold to the magistrate as a reward for his expenses.'
  • 及起马，令来道谢叩送，将军赞叹不绝而去 - Literally 'when the horse is ready to leave, the magistrate comes to thank and bow farewell, the general praises continuously as he leaves,' meaning 'when the general is ready to depart, the magistrate comes to thank and bow farewell, and the general praises continuously as he leaves.'
  • 膺保荐，仕至宪司 - Literally 'receive recommendation and serve up to the position of Inspector General,' meaning 'receive a recommendation and rise to the position of Inspector General.'
  • 亲力 - Literally 'personal effort,' meaning 'personal merit.'
  • 才能之士，湮没无闻 - Literally 'talented people, buried and unheard of,' meaning 'talented people who are overlooked and forgotten.'
  • 惜乎 - A classical expression of regret, literally 'alas' or 'how pitiful.'
  • 小焉者也 - Literally 'a small matter,' meaning 'a minor issue.'
  • 史不列其名姓 - Literally 'history does not record their names,' meaning 'history does not mention their names.'

  • 金之两书生 - Literally 'two scholars of Jin,' referring to historical figures.

  • 怨恸于九泉 - Literally 'grieve in the netherworld,' meaning 'suffer in the afterlife.'
  • 不屑不洁，如遗逸辈之特隐其名 - Literally 'disdain to be unclean, like the recluses who hide their names,' meaning 'disdain to be involved in corruption, like the recluses who hide their names.'
  • 但愿我儿愚且鲁，无灾无害到公卿 - Literally 'only wish my son to be foolish and dull, without disaster or harm to become a high official,' meaning 'I only wish my son to be simple and unambitious, without disaster or harm to become a high official.'
  • 固不屑为公卿 - Literally 'they certainly do not desire to be high officials,' meaning 'they certainly do not desire to be high officials.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 清兵入，戡定大难 - Refers to the Qing army's intervention to restore order during a period of chaos.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This long sentence describes a series of actions taken by the old man to create the illusion of a fire. It must be read as a sequence of events.
  • This sentence marks the passage of time and the return of the bandits, followed by the old man's emotional response and explanation.
  • This sentence describes the sequence of events leading to the restoration of order and the establishment of a legitimate ruler.
  • This sentence introduces the educational achievements of the old man's sons.
  • This sentence provides background on the marriage and the wife's literary skills.
  • This sentence describes the wife's interest in her husband's studies, her evaluation of his writing, and her attribution of the problem to the teacher.
  • This sentence shows the wife's confidence in her ability to improve the writing.
  • This sentence describes the husband's reaction and the scholars' comments on his progress.
  • This sentence describes the talents of the younger brothers and their predicted success in the imperial examinations.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 翁惊讶失色 - Describes a state of shock and fear, where the old man's face turns pale with surprise.

  • 但窃取之物，不但污我品行，且恐失物之家，冤及婢仆，性命攸关，奈何 - A complex sentence expressing concern over the moral and practical implications of the cat's actions, emphasizing the potential harm to the family's reputation and the servants.
  • 井上之李，岂无主者 - A rhetorical question suggesting that even a seemingly ownerless object (like a plum) has an owner.
  • 廉士尚且取之 - Refers to the idea that even a person of high moral standing might take something out of necessity.
  • 所谓饥不择食也 - A common saying meaning 'when hungry, one doesn't pick and choose.'
  • 况此物自至，必天神怜翁，假手以济 - Suggests that the appearance of the item is a sign of divine assistance.
  • 无已，姑先质资度岁 - Means 'if there is no other choice, we will temporarily pawn the item to get through the year.'
  • 暗访物主，明告其故而归以质券 - Suggests a plan to find the owner discreetly and return the item with a pawn ticket as proof of good faith.
  • 似亦无伤 - Means 'it seems harmless.'
  • 次年遍访，无失物家 - Indicates that the next year, they searched for the owner without success.
  • 此巨家殉葬物，年久墓崩，家贫棺坏，则猫取之矣 - Suggests that the item might have been part of a burial offering, taken by the cat due to the tomb's collapse and the family's poverty.
  • 有心计妇，家有荡子，藏此物于复壁承尘中，为子女谋 - Suggests that a clever woman might have hidden the item for her children's future.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 翁 - Refers to the old man, a common term for an elderly male.
  • 妻女 - Refers to the old man's wife and daughter, indicating a family unit.
  • 猫 - Refers to a cat, which in Chinese culture is often seen as a clever and sometimes mischievous animal.
  • 井上之李 - Refers to plums on a well, a common image in Chinese literature and poetry.
  • 廉士 - Refers to a person of high moral integrity.
  • 饥不择食 - A common saying in Chinese culture, emphasizing the importance of survival over moral considerations.
  • 天神 - Refers to divine beings, reflecting the belief in supernatural assistance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '翁惊讶失色，一喜一惧，曰：“猫虽通灵，但窃取之物，不但污我品行，且恐失物之家，冤及婢仆，性命攸关，奈何？”' is a complex dialogue, where the old man expresses his mixed feelings about the cat's actions and their consequences.
  • The sentence '其妻女曰：“翁言虽是，但井上之李，岂无主者，廉士尚且取之，所谓饥不择食也。况此物自至，必天神怜翁，假手以济，岂尽狸奴力耶？无已，姑先质资度岁，暗访物主，明告其故而归以质券，似亦无伤。”' is a response from the old man's wife and daughter, presenting a logical argument for why the item should be kept and how to handle the situation.
  • The sequence '翁不得已，姑从之。次年遍访，无失物家。或曰：“此巨家殉葬物，年久墓崩，家贫棺坏，则猫取之矣。”或曰：“有心计妇，家有荡子，藏此物于复壁承尘中，为子女谋。”' describes the old man's reluctant agreement, the subsequent search for the owner, and two possible explanations for the item's origin.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 神天赏善之说 - Refers to the belief in divine rewards for good deeds, a common theme in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 起家 - In the context of establishing a household, this term reflects the importance of family lineage and inheritance in traditional Chinese society.
  • 子孙发甲 - This phrase emphasizes the importance of offspring and the continuation of the family line.
  • 爱畜猫 - Cats were often kept as pets in traditional Chinese households, and this practice is mentioned here.
  • 食必以腥 - Feeding cats with meat is a specific detail that highlights the care given to these animals.
  • 仕至宪司者 - This refers to a high-ranking official, indicating the social status and influence of the individual.
  • 署中猫且数十头 - The presence of numerous cats in an official's office suggests a personal interest in or fondness for cats.
  • 出入随从 - This phrase indicates a level of service or attention given to the cats.
  • 专有饲猫之职 - The establishment of a specific role for feeding cats shows the importance placed on this activity.
  • 至今不衰 - This phrase suggests that the tradition of keeping cats has persisted over time.
  • 人生世上，财可忽乎哉 - This rhetorical question reflects the traditional Chinese view of the importance of wealth and material success.
  • 庭帏行孝，棣萼情联 - These phrases emphasize the values of filial piety and familial harmony, which are central to traditional Chinese ethics.
  • 爵可得而鬻也，刑可得而赎焉 - This statement touches on the corrupt practices of the time, where titles and punishments could be bought and sold.
  • 以之救济，仁名顿起 - This phrase highlights the positive social impact of charitable actions.
  • 以之施与，传为美谈 - This phrase emphasizes the lasting legacy of generosity and kindness.
  • 名以银成，无别术也 - This statement reflects the cynical view that fame can be bought, a common theme in literature and discourse.

  • 今见其勤俭，能自经营 - Statement about the nephew's current frugality and ability to manage his own affairs, where '勤俭' means 'diligent and thrifty,' and '自经营' means 'to manage on one's own.'
  • 我何敢负李兄于地下乎 - Statement of moral obligation, where '何敢' means 'how dare,' and '负李兄于地下' means 'betray Li's brother in the afterlife.'
  • 乃出李子所开单目 - Phrase indicating the narrator's action of presenting a list, where '单目' means 'a list' or 'a record.'
  • 请诸公作主匀分 - Request for others to decide on the fair distribution, where '作主' means 'to decide' or 'to arbitrate,' and '匀分' means 'to distribute evenly.'
  • 诸公且止，听我一言 - Request for others to pause and listen to the narrator's explanation, where '且止' means 'please stop,' and '听我一言' means 'listen to what I have to say.'
  • 赵伯所云，并无凭证，是欲为义士耳 - Statement about the lack of evidence and the narrator's intention to act honorably, where '并无凭证' means 'no proof,' and '欲为义士' means 'to act as a righteous person.'
  • 侄虽年幼，亦不敢取非义财 - Statement about the nephew's refusal to accept unjustly gained wealth, where '虽年幼' means 'although young,' and '非义财' means 'unjustly gained wealth.'
  • 即亡父果存百千钱，以远年债，一本一利，取二百贯足矣 - Statement about the nephew's calculation of a fair amount, where '亡父果存百千钱' means 'if my father had indeed left behind a hundred thousand coins,' '远年债' means 'old debts,' '一本一利' means 'principal and interest,' and '取二百贯足矣' means 'taking two hundred strings of cash would be enough.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 姑苏 - Classical name for Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu province, often used in classical literature to refer to the region or its people.
  • 富而无嗣 - Wealthy but without descendants, a common expression in classical Chinese to describe a person who is prosperous but childless.
  • 及笄 - A term referring to a girl reaching the age of 15, traditionally marked by the ceremony of tying up her hair with a hairpin, signifying her coming of age.
  • 病笃 - Severe illness, used to describe a condition that is very serious or critical.
  • 医皆束手 - All doctors gave up, meaning that even the best medical professionals were unable to treat the illness.
  • 聘名医叶天士诊之 - Hired the famous doctor Ye Tienshi to diagnose her, with *叶天士* being a historical figure, a renowned physician of the Qing dynasty.
  • 非病也 - It is not an illness, indicating that the condition is not a physical disease but something else.
  • 肯以若女为我女 - If you are willing to let your daughter be my daughter, a conditional statement involving a transfer of guardianship or adoption.
  • 且从我游 - And follow me on my travels, implying that the girl will be taken under the doctor's care for a period of time.
  • 百日后，还阁下以壮健者 - After a hundred days, I will return to you a strong and healthy daughter, a promise that the girl will be cured or transformed.
  • 非复娇弱之态矣 - No longer in a delicate and weak state, indicating a complete recovery or transformation.
  • 如迟疑不决，是翁自杀之 - If you hesitate, it will be the old man who kills himself, a dramatic statement indicating that the father's hesitation would lead to his own death.
  • 死非正命，良可哀也 - To die not by a proper fate is indeed very pitiable, a moral judgment that dying due to a preventable cause is tragic.
  • 诧曰 - Surprised and said, indicating the father's reaction to the doctor's proposal.
  • 诚如是，愿以千金送阁下 - If it is truly as you say, I am willing to give you a thousand gold pieces, a formal expression of agreement and reward.
  • 携归 - To take her back, indicating the doctor taking the girl to his home.
  • 另洁密室 - To prepare a clean, private room, suggesting a special, secluded space for the girl.
  • 选婢之美而艳者 - Select a beautiful and attractive female servant, indicating the choice of a companion for the girl.
  • 使伴女宿 - To have her stay with the girl at night, indicating a close, constant presence.
  • 嘱曰 - He instructed her, saying, introducing a direct quotation or instruction.
  • 此汝姑也 - This is your aunt, indicating the servant is now to be treated as a maternal figure.
  • 终身依倚在是 - You will rely on her for life, indicating a lifelong dependency or relationship.
  • 顺姑无违 - You must obey her and not disobey, a command to the girl to be obedient.
  • 稍有拂逆，致增其病 - If there is the slightest disobedience, it will worsen her condition, suggesting that the girl's behavior directly affects her health.
  • 唯汝是问 - Only you will be held responsible, indicating that the servant is accountable for the girl's well-being.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 姑苏 - Refers to Suzhou, a historically significant city in Jiangsu province, known for its cultural and literary heritage.
  • 叶天士 - A historical figure, Ye Tienshi (1661–1740), a renowned physician of the Qing dynasty, known for his contributions to traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 及笄 - A traditional Chinese rite of passage for girls at age 15, signifying their eligibility for marriage.
  • 病笃 - Reflects the classical Chinese medical tradition of diagnosing and treating severe illnesses, often with a focus on both physical and spiritual causes.
  • 医皆束手 - Indicates the limits of traditional Chinese medicine, where even the best doctors could not cure certain conditions, often attributed to supernatural or emotional causes.
  • 聘名医 - Shows the high value placed on medical expertise in classical Chinese society, especially for the wealthy.
  • 百日后 - A traditional time period in Chinese medicine and mysticism, often associated with the completion of a transformation or healing process.
  • 非复娇弱之态矣 - Suggests a belief in the possibility of transformation, not only physical but also emotional or spiritual, which was a common theme in classical Chinese narratives.
  • 死非正命 - Reflects the classical Chinese concept of *ming* (命), or fate, where dying due to misfortune or failure to act is seen as a tragic deviation from one's proper destiny.
  • 千金 - A large sum of money, often used in classical literature to indicate wealth or a significant reward.
  • 密室 - A private, secluded room, often used in classical Chinese literature for scenes of transformation, seclusion, or mystical practices.
  • 婢之美而艳者 - Reflects the classical Chinese preference for beauty and the role of women in both domestic and mystical contexts.
  • 姑 - In classical Chinese, '姑' can mean 'aunt' or 'maternal aunt,' but in this context, it is used to denote a role or relationship, not a blood relation.
  • 终身依倚 - Reflects the traditional Chinese emphasis on familial and social roles, where dependency is a key aspect of relationships.

Proper Nouns

  • 姑苏 - Suzhou (a city in Jiangsu Province, China)
  • 叶天士 - Ye Tienshi (a historical physician of the Qing dynasty)
  • 百日 - A traditional time period of 100 days, often used in Chinese medical and mystical contexts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 天士 - Likely a reference to **Tian Shi** (田士), a character or historical figure, possibly a physician, as the text discusses medical transformation.
  • 婢 - A female servant or maid.
  • 姑 - A term for 'mistress' or 'aunt' in a family context, here likely referring to a married woman, possibly the wife of the father.
  • 翁 - A term for 'old man' or 'father,' referring to the patriarch of the family.
  • 义女 - A 'daughter-in-law' or 'adopted daughter,' depending on context; in this case, it seems to be a deceptive claim.
  • 伪疾 - 'False illness' or 'faked illness,' indicating deceit or deception.
  • 同衾共枕 - A phrase from classical literature, often used to indicate a romantic or marital relationship.
  • 弓足 - Refers to **bound feet**, a traditional Chinese practice for women.
  • 衣冠履舄 - Refers to **traditional male attire**, including ceremonial shoes (*舄*).
  • 《医经》 - The **Medical Classic**, likely referring to classical Chinese medical texts such as the *Huangdi Neijing* (Yellow Emperor’s Inner Canon) or other foundational medical works.
  • 五不男 - Refers to the **five types of 'not-male'** or individuals with incomplete male characteristics, a concept from classical Chinese medical texts.

  • 绝不通问 - Means 'she never communicated with me again,' indicating a complete severance of communication.
  • 故不及详知其家世也 - Means 'so I did not get to know her family background in detail,' indicating a lack of information.
  • 默然良久 - Means 'remained silent for a long time,' indicating a period of contemplation.
  • 出访之 - Means 'went out to inquire about her,' indicating an attempt to find her.
  • 杳无影响 - Means 'no trace or news,' indicating a lack of information.
  • 如是者已历二年 - Means 'such was the case for two years,' indicating the duration.
  • 讵料嫁者不成为嫁，死者亦不成为死 - Means 'who could have thought that the remarried one was not truly remarried, and the dead one was not truly dead,' indicating a surprising revelation.
  • 癸酉年三月 - Means 'in the third month of the year Guiyou,' specifying a particular year and month in the sexagenary cycle.
  • 某生客帆归省 - Means 'a certain guest returned home to visit,' indicating a return visit.
  • 行李焕然 - Means 'his luggage was fresh and new,' indicating a well-prepared appearance.
  • 卸装后 - Means 'after unloading his luggage,' indicating the completion of his preparations.
  • 抵岳家视妻 - Means 'went to his father-in-law's house to see his wife,' indicating a visit.
  • 则妻不获面 - Means 'but he did not see his wife,' indicating a failure to meet.
  • 妻兄作贾 - Means 'his wife's brother was a merchant,' indicating his occupation.
  • 妻嫂亦已下世 - Means 'his wife's sister-in-law had also passed away,' indicating a death.
  • 只余小舅小姨辈 - Means 'only the younger brother-in-law and younger sister-in-law remained,' indicating the surviving family members.
  • 问及其妻 - Means 'they asked about his wife,' indicating an inquiry.
  • 小舅言已经改嫁 - Means 'the younger brother-in-law said she had remarried,' indicating a new marriage.
  • 叩其究竟 - Means 'asked for details,' indicating a request for more information.

  • 生遂辞出 - Means 'the guest then left,' indicating his departure.
  • 未几妻兄自贾所归 - Means 'not long after, the wife's brother returned from his business trip,' indicating his return.
  • 弟妹告以姊丈固未死 - Means 'the younger brother and sister informed him that his elder brother-in-law was not dead,' indicating a revelation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 迄乎酒阑灯尽 - Classical phrase meaning 'until the wine is finished and the lamps are extinguished,' indicating the end of a drinking party.
  • 话及当年事 - Means 'when talking about past events.'
  • 相对如梦寐焉 - Poetic expression meaning 'they looked at each other as if in a dream.'
  • 覆水难收 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'once water is spilled, it cannot be collected again,' symbolizing an irreversible situation.
  • 顾卿何以自处 - Means 'how should you deal with yourself?'
  • 覆水难收，君即不见责，然势难再合 - Combines the metaphor of spilled water with the idea that even if the man does not blame his wife, their situation makes reconciliation impossible.
  • 此我之罪，与卿无干 - Means 'this is my fault, not yours.'
  • 乃相与坐叹 - Means 'they sat together and sighed.'
  • 至晓，生暂别 - Means 'until dawn, the man temporarily parted.'
  • 归思所以策其后者 - Means 'he returned to think about how to deal with the situation later.'
  • 临别时，妻曰：“君于午后须再至，作此生终见之缘。” - Means 'when parting, the wife said, 'You must come back after noon, as this will be our final meeting.''
  • 生以抱恨成猝病，不能如约 - Means 'the man, burdened by his sorrow, fell ill suddenly and could not keep the appointment.'
  • 迨相隔七八日再往，则已玉暗香消 - Means 'after being apart for several days, when he went back, she was already gone, her beauty and fragrance faded.'
  • 询其死由，鸨母含涕，谓“乃仰洋药以死者。” - Means 'when he asked about the cause of her death, the madam wept and said, 'She died from taking opium.''
  • 伊无欠负人家债目，又非有所格而不能遂，实未审其致命之故也 - Means 'She owed no debts to anyone, nor was there anything preventing her from continuing her life; the true cause of her death remains unknown.'
  • 某惊惶无语，唯问其葬所 - Means 'He was shocked and speechless, only asking about her burial place.'
  • 俟问土匠，始知之 - Means 'After asking the grave diggers, he finally learned.'
  • 生归，叹人世情缘，甚于梦幻泡影 - Means 'When he returned, he lamented that human relationships are more fleeting than dreams, illusions, or bubbles.'
  • 拟挥慧剑，断烦恼丝 - Means 'He intended to use a wisdom sword to cut through his troubles.'
  • 一切达观 - Means 'to accept everything with equanimity.'
  • 但愿有酒浇愁，任歌得意 - Means 'He just wished to drown his sorrows in wine and sing to his heart's content.'
  • 若以无稽诮我，宛在斯人 - Means 'If someone were to criticize me for this, they would be like this person.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 酒阑灯尽 - Classical description of the end of a drinking party.
  • 覆水难收 - Well-known Chinese idiom symbolizing an irreversible situation.
  • 洋药 - Refers to opium, a substance that was introduced to China during the Qing dynasty and became a significant social issue.
  • 鸨母 - Refers to the madam of a brothel, often depicted as a powerful and sometimes sympathetic figure in classical Chinese literature.
  • 慧剑 - Metaphor for wisdom or insight, often used in Buddhist contexts to symbolize the cutting away of ignorance and delusion.
  • 梦幻泡影 - Buddhist concept referring to the impermanence and illusory nature of life.
  • 达观 - Philosophical attitude of accepting everything with equanimity, often associated with Buddhist or Taoist thought.
  • 无稽 - Means 'baseless' or 'groundless,' often used to describe unfounded accusations or criticisms.

  • 曼卿 - Zhang Hua (张华), a famous scholar and official of the Jin Dynasty, known for his stories and legends.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 而先生以為根浮壽短，何所見之疏也 - Rhetorical question criticizing the speaker’s view that the flower has a shallow root and a short lifespan; '根浮壽短' is a metaphor for fragility or impermanence; '何所見之疏也' means 'how narrow or shallow is your understanding?'
  • 吾不揣冒昧 - Modest expression meaning 'I dare not presume, but I will speak boldly.'
  • 命彼花神 - 'Command the flower spirit' – invoking a supernatural being or deity of flowers.
  • 人煙辐辏之區 - 'A place where many people gather' or 'a bustling, densely populated area'; '辐辏' is a metaphor from the wheel, where spokes converge.
  • 為世俗一新耳目 - 'To refresh the eyes and ears of the common people' – to bring a new and fresh perspective to the masses.
  • 俾知华之质，亦足以寄傲风霜 - 'To make them know that the essence of the *tianhua* (heavenly flower) is sufficient to defy wind and frost'; '寄傲风霜' is a metaphor for resilience in adversity.
  • 艳丽之姿，何不可争芳松竹 - 'Its beautiful form, how could it not vie in beauty with the pine and bamboo?' A metaphor comparing the flower’s beauty to the enduring virtues of pine and bamboo.
  • 置夔龙于叔世，岂无逆鲠之忠 - 'To place the virtuous and capable (like Kuai and Long) in a corrupt age—how could there not be loyal and upright ministers?' A reference to the idea that talent and virtue may be overlooked in an unjust time.
  • 嫁钟郝于蓬门，讵少摩笄之节 - 'To marry the virtuous (Zhong and Hao) to a humble family—how could there be a lack of noble virtue?' '摩笄' refers to a symbol of female virtue and loyalty, possibly from the story of Lady Xiangjun.
  • 桃花之不与菊梅比烈者，不为也，非不能也 - 'The peach blossom does not compare in intensity to chrysanthemum and plum—this is not because it cannot, but because it chooses not to.' A metaphor for the different roles or purposes of different flowers.
  • 时不同也 - 'It is because the time is different.' Emphasizes the importance of context or era.
  • 若谓化工嘘拂，亦其福命使然 - 'If you say that the transformation of nature (the creative force of heaven) is due to the flower’s good fortune and destiny.'
  • 彼寂寞空山，凄凉篱下者，时运之不逮，又何忮乎他人哉 - 'Those who are lonely in the empty mountains, or desolate in the fence—these are those who have not had the fortune of their time. How can they resent others?' A philosophical statement on fate and resentment.
  • 悠悠之言，初无足责 - 'Such idle talk is not worth taking seriously.' A dismissal of superficial or baseless criticism.
  • 先生灵根未泯，慧果犹存 - 'Your spiritual root is not yet extinguished, and your wisdom still remains.' A poetic way of saying that the speaker still has insight and understanding.
  • 慎勿以褊见迂词，令彼名花抱恨也 - 'Be careful not to let narrow-mindedness and obscure words cause the famous flower to suffer in sorrow.' A plea not to judge or misunderstand the flower’s purpose or value.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 花神 - 'Flower spirit' or 'flower deity,' a supernatural being in Chinese tradition who governs the blooming of flowers.
  • 重阳 - The Double Ninth Festival, a traditional Chinese festival celebrated on the ninth day of the ninth lunar month, associated with longevity and climbing.
  • 小春 - A term referring to the early spring season, or the time just after winter.
  • 夔龙 - Mythical beings representing virtue and power. Kuai is a one-legged dragon, and Long is a dragon; they are often used to symbolize talent and moral strength.
  • 钟郝 - Refers to Zhong and Hao, likely referring to Zhong You (钟繇) and Hao Xian (郝吉), but more likely a poetic reference to virtuous figures or couples.
  • 摩笄 - A term possibly referring to the story of Lady Xiangjun (香君), who is said to have cut off her hair and pierced her cheek with a jade comb (摩笄) as a symbol of loyalty and virtue.
  • 桃花 - The peach blossom, often symbolizing spring, beauty, and transience in Chinese poetry.
  • 菊梅 - Chrysanthemum and plum blossom, both symbols of endurance and resilience in Chinese culture.
  • 化工 - The creative force of nature or heaven, often used in classical Chinese to refer to the natural transformation or divine will.
  • 福命 - 'Good fortune and destiny,' a concept in Chinese philosophy and Confucian thought.
  • 蓬门 - A humble or poor family, often used metaphorically for a modest or low-status household.
  • 时运之不逮 - 'The lack of favorable timing or fortune.' A common expression in classical Chinese to explain misfortune.
  • 忮 - To resent or envy, often used in the context of moral or philosophical reflection.
  • 灵根 - 'Spiritual root' or 'inner essence,' a term used in Daoist and Buddhist thought to refer to one's innate spiritual nature.
  • 慧果 - 'Wisdom fruit,' a metaphor for wisdom or enlightenment.
  • 褊见 - 'Narrow-minded view' or 'limited perspective.'

  • 已同奔月嫦娥 - Meaning 'already become like Chang'e who flew to the moon.' '嫦娥' is the name of the moon goddess who flew to the moon after consuming an elixir of immortality.

  • 适逢其偶 - Meaning 'happened to meet the right person.' '适逢' means 'happened to meet,' and '其偶' means 'the right person.'
  • 某之贸贸然来 - Meaning 'I came carelessly.' '贸贸然' means 'carelessly' or 'recklessly.'
  • 欲即反离 - Meaning 'wanted to come and then leave.' '欲' means 'wanted,' '即' means 'to come,' and '反离' means 'to leave.'

  • 假手于某 - Meaning 'using me as a tool.' '假手' means 'using someone as a tool,' and '于某' means 'with me.'
  • 以为从陈地步 - Meaning 'thinking it was a way to follow Chen.' '以为' means 'thinking,' '从' means 'following,' and '陈地步' means 'Chen's path.'
  • 初本疑信参半 - Meaning 'initially, doubt and belief were equal.' '初本' means 'initially,' '疑信' means 'doubt and belief,' and '参半' means 'equal.'
  • 有识陈者 - Meaning 'those who know Chen.' '有识' means 'those who know,' and '陈' refers to Chen Sheng.
  • 见陈于数日前 - Meaning 'saw Chen a few days ago.' '见' means 'saw,' '于' means 'at,' and '数日前' means 'a few days ago.'
  • 略无疑义云 - Meaning 'slightly without doubt.' '略' means 'slightly,' '无疑义' means 'without doubt,' and '云' is a particle used to introduce a statement.
  • 情波变幻 - Meaning 'emotional waves changing.' '情波' means 'emotional waves,' and '变幻' means 'changing.'
  • 欲海迷离 - Meaning 'the sea of desire is bewildering.' '欲海' means 'the sea of desire,' and '迷离' means 'bewildering.'
  • 月地花天 - Meaning 'moonlit ground and flower-filled sky.' '月地' means 'moonlit ground,' and '花天' means 'flower-filled sky.'
  • 过于蜃楼海市 - Meaning 'exceeding the mirages of the sea.' '过于' means 'exceeding,' '蜃楼' means 'mirage,' and '海市' means 'sea market' or 'sea mirage.'
  • 走马王孙 - Meaning 'young nobles riding horses.' '走马' means 'riding horses,' and '王孙' means 'young nobles.'

  • 尚无错于用情 - Meaning 'still not wrong in using emotions.' '尚' means 'still,' '无错' means 'not wrong,' and '用情' means 'using emotions.'
